title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Modern DSM pipelines routinely conflate variable importance causal effect, despite mature statistical apparatus — Pearl’s structural causal models (Pearl 2009; Pearl, Glymour, Jewell 2016) — distinguishes two cleanly. Pillar 1 edaphos ships minimum-viable interface apparatus: pedogenetic Directed Acyclic Graphs (DAGs) expressed dagitty syntax (Textor et al. 2016), automatic backdoor-adjustment set identification, linear causal-effect estimator confounded-baseline comparator. vignette demonstrates pipeline bundled Cerrado dataset highlights direction future extensions toward causal representation learning (Schölkopf et al. 2021) time-series causal discovery (Runge et al. 2019).

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Consider generating process br_cerrado: elevation influences slope mean annual precipitation; slope precipitation influence Topographic Wetness Index (TWI) NDVI; five variables jointly influence topsoil SOC. ordinary regression lm(soc∼ndvi)\mathrm{lm}(\text{soc}\sim\text{ndvi}) recover direct effect NDVI SOC: sums direct path backdoor paths TWI, slope precipitation (Pearl 2009, Ch. 3). Backdoor adjustment textbook fix: condition set variables blocks every backdoor path without opening collider path. Given DAG, dagitty::adjustmentSets() returns valid sets automatically (Textor et al. 2016).

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: [causal_cerrado_dag()][causal_cerrado_dag] encodes generating process br_cerrado.
code: library(edaphos) if (!requireNamespace("dagitty", quietly = TRUE)) {   knitr::knit_exit("Package `dagitty` not installed — skipping vignette.") } g <- causal_cerrado_dag() g #> dag { #> elev #> map_mm #> ndvi #> slope #> soc #> twi #> elev -> map_mm #> elev -> slope #> elev -> soc #> elev -> twi #> map_mm -> ndvi #> map_mm -> soc #> map_mm -> twi #> ndvi -> soc #> slope -> ndvi #> slope -> soc #> slope -> twi #> twi -> ndvi #> twi -> soc #> } plot(g) #> Plot coordinates for graph not supplied! Generating coordinates, see ?coordinates for how to set your own.

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: minimal adjustment set (computed ) blocks every backdoor path ndvi soc leaving direct ndvi -> soc edge unconditioned — standard back-door criterion result (Pearl 2009).
code: adj <- causal_adjustment_set(g, exposure = "ndvi", outcome = "soc") adj #> [1] "map_mm" "slope"  "twi"

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: wrapper [causal_estimate_effect()][causal_estimate_effect] fits adjusted linear model soc=β0+βndvindvi+∑z∈Zγzz+ε, \mathrm{soc}\;=\;\beta_{0}+\beta_{\text{ndvi}}\,\mathrm{ndvi}                  +\sum_{z\Z}\gamma_{z}\,z+\varepsilon,  ZZ returned adjustment set. also reports confounded-baseline coefficient βndvinaive\beta_{\text{ndvi}}^{\text{naive}} obtained unadjusted lm(soc∼ndvi)\mathrm{lm}(\text{soc}\sim\text{ndvi}), magnitude confounding bias directly legible.
code: data(br_cerrado, package = "edaphos") fit <- causal_estimate_effect(   br_cerrado, g,   exposure = "ndvi", outcome = "soc",   effect   = "direct" ) fit #> <edaphos_causal_effect> #>   ndvi -> soc   (estimator: lm) #>   adjustment set : {map_mm, slope, twi} #>   direct effect  : 18.43   (95% CI: 14.61, 22.25) #>   naive effect   : 49.36   (un-adjusted, likely confounded) library(ggplot2) df <- data.frame(   estimator = factor(c("Naive (lm(soc ~ ndvi))",                         "Adjusted (backdoor)"),                       levels = c("Naive (lm(soc ~ ndvi))",                                   "Adjusted (backdoor)")),   coef      = c(fit$effect_naive, fit$effect) ) ggplot(df, aes(estimator, coef, fill = estimator)) +   geom_col(width = 0.6) +   geom_text(aes(label = signif(coef, 3)), vjust = -0.3) +   labs(y = "Estimated effect of NDVI on SOC",        title = "NDVI -> SOC — naive vs. causal (Cerrado DAG)") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +   theme(legend.position = "none")

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: problems beyond br_cerrado, [causal_clorpt_dag()][causal_clorpt_dag] returns classical Jenny (1941) factorial DAG template (Jenny 1941; McBratney, Mendonça Santos, Minasny 2003):
code: g_clorpt <- causal_clorpt_dag() g_clorpt #> dag { #> CEC #> Clay #> Climate #> Erosion #> Organisms #> ParentMaterial #> Relief #> SOC #> TWI #> Time #> Weathering #> pH #> Clay -> CEC #> Climate -> Organisms #> Climate -> SOC #> Climate -> Weathering #> Erosion -> SOC #> Organisms -> SOC #> ParentMaterial -> Clay #> ParentMaterial -> Weathering #> Relief -> Erosion #> Relief -> TWI #> SOC -> CEC #> TWI -> SOC #> Time -> SOC #> Time -> Weathering #> Weathering -> Clay #> Weathering -> pH #> }

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: expert-written DAG encodes single analyst’s view pedogenesis. defensible structural model reflect wider published literature — manually encoding every causal claim hundreds papers infeasible. Pillar 1 therefore ships three-stage LLM + Knowledge-Graph (KG) pipeline automates transition corpus abstracts augmented DAG: Three interchangeable backends supported behind single extractor: "ollama" — local, zero-cost. Default model "gemma4:latest" (9.6 GB, e4b); switch "gemma4:26b" higher extraction fidelity. backend used produce snapshot shipped inst/extdata/cerrado_claims.jsonl. "openai" — hosted GPT family JSON mode. Requires OPENAI_API_KEY. "anthropic" — Claude Messages API. Requires ANTHROPIC_API_KEY.
code: abstracts  ->  causal_llm_extract()  ->  tidy claims  ->  causal_kg_*  ->  causal_augment_dag()   (corpus)     (Gemma-4 / GPT-5 / Claude)   (data frame)    (KG)          (augmented DAG)

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Ten curated Cerrado-pedology abstracts ship inst/extdata/cerrado_abstracts.jsonl, together Gemma-4 extractions ready load inst/extdata/cerrado_claims.jsonl. Loading cached JSON lets vignette build offline, reproducibly, even CI runners Ollama.
code: if (!requireNamespace("igraph", quietly = TRUE)) {   knitr::knit_exit("Package `igraph` not installed — skipping KG section.") } abs_path <- system.file("extdata", "cerrado_abstracts.jsonl",                         package = "edaphos") claim_path <- system.file("extdata", "cerrado_claims.jsonl",                           package = "edaphos")  abstracts <- do.call(rbind, lapply(readLines(abs_path), function(l) {   j <- jsonlite::fromJSON(l)   data.frame(source = j$source, abstract = j$abstract,              stringsAsFactors = FALSE) })) claims <- do.call(rbind, lapply(readLines(claim_path), function(l) {   as.data.frame(jsonlite::fromJSON(l), stringsAsFactors = FALSE) })) nrow(abstracts); nrow(claims) #> [1] 10 #> [1] 30 head(claims[, c("cause", "effect", "source", "confidence")], 5) #>                                 cause                      effect #> 1           mean_annual_precipitation                         soc #> 2           mean_annual_precipitation        above_ground_biomass #> 3           mean_annual_precipitation               decomposition #> 4 time_since_parent_material_exposure primary_mineral_dissolution #> 5                         temperature primary_mineral_dissolution #>                             source confidence #> 1 Ferreira2021_Cerrado_SOC_climate        0.9 #> 2 Ferreira2021_Cerrado_SOC_climate        0.9 #> 3 Ferreira2021_Cerrado_SOC_climate        0.9 #> 4     Oliveira2019_Oxisol_argillic        0.9 #> 5     Oliveira2019_Oxisol_argillic        0.9

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Ollama server available localhost:11434, claims recovered end--end. Guard chunk vignette still reproducible machine without Ollama:
code: kg_live <- causal_kg_new() kg_live <- causal_llm_ingest_corpus(   kg_live, abstracts,   abstract_col = "abstract",   source_col   = "source",   backend      = "ollama",   model        = "gemma4:latest",   min_confidence = 0.5,   progress = function(i, n, s) message(sprintf("[%d/%d] %s", i, n, s)) ) kg_live

what: 6.3 Building the KG from the cached claims
title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
code: kg <- causal_kg_new() for (i in seq_len(nrow(claims))) {   kg <- causal_kg_add_edge(     kg,     cause      = claims$cause[i],     effect     = claims$effect[i],     source     = claims$source[i],     evidence   = claims$evidence[i],     confidence = claims$confidence[i]   ) } kg #> <edaphos_causal_kg> #>   nodes : 41 #>   edges : 30 #>   confidence: min = 0.90, median = 0.90, max = 0.90 #>   DAG        : yes

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: causal_augment_dag() takes expert base DAG unions every KG edge whose confidence >= min_confidence. Edges introduce cycle — break backdoor identifiability — rejected warning.
code: g_aug <- causal_augment_dag(g, kg, min_confidence = 0.7) diff <- causal_augment_diff(g, g_aug) table(diff$origin) #>  #> base   kg  #>   13   29 head(diff[diff$origin == "kg", ], 8) #>               cause                             effect origin #> 1  argillic_horizon effective_cation_exchange_capacity     kg #> 2      clay_content     plant_available_water_capacity     kg #> 3      clay_content                    water_retention     kg #> 8           erosion                           soc_loss     kg #> 9    fire_frequency                        topsoil_soc     kg #> 10         land_use                                soc     kg #> 11           liming                        available_p     kg #> 12           liming                    exchangeable_ca     kg

what: 6.5 Re-estimating the effect with the augmented DAG
title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: literature agrees previously-ignored variable (erosion, say) confounds ndvi -> soc, augmented DAG add minimal adjustment set, point estimate direct effect may shift materially. remainder analysis — fitting adjusted model, comparing naive estimator — unchanged: DAG just richer.
code: adj_base <- causal_adjustment_set(g,     exposure = "ndvi", outcome = "soc") adj_aug  <- tryCatch(   causal_adjustment_set(g_aug, exposure = "ndvi", outcome = "soc"),   error = function(e) adj_base ) list(base = adj_base, augmented = adj_aug) #> $base #> [1] "map_mm" "slope"  "twi"    #>  #> $augmented #> [1] "map_mm" "slope"  "twi"

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: LLM output labels free-form — one abstract produces steeper_slopes, another slope_angle, third slope. Knowledge Graph fuses classical DAG, synonymous labels must collapse single canonical term. Pillar 1 ships three-tier matcher — exact, substring, fuzzy Levenshtein — hand-curated Cerrado-pedometry vocabulary (~60 terms, subset AGROVOC + ENVO): Live SPARQL queries FAO AGROVOC endpoint (causal_ontology_agrovoc()) parsing local ENVO .obo file (causal_ontology_envo(), via optional ontologyIndex Suggests) exposed problems whose vocabulary extends beyond Cerrado core.
code: mapping <- causal_kg_alignment(kg) head(mapping[mapping$method != "exact", ], 8) #>                               original       canonical    method distance #> 3                 above_ground_biomass         biomass substring       NA #> 5  time_since_parent_material_exposure parent_material substring       NA #> 6          primary_mineral_dissolution            <NA>      none       NA #> 9           clay_content_at_bt_horizon            clay substring       NA #> 10                    argillic_horizon            <NA>      none       NA #> 11  effective_cation_exchange_capacity            <NA>      none       NA #> 13         surface_layer_soil_moisture   soil_moisture substring       NA #> 16                          root_input            <NA>      none       NA kg_canon <- causal_kg_rename(kg, mapping) kg_canon #> <edaphos_causal_kg> #>   nodes : 33 #>   edges : 29 #>   confidence: min = 0.90, median = 0.90, max = 0.90 #>   DAG        : yes

what: 7. Corpus ingestion from SciELO and OpenAlex
title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Two zero-account clients turn literature search queries abstract-ready data frames can piped directly [causal_llm_ingest_corpus()][causal_llm_ingest_corpus]: clients return identical column schemas (source, title, abstract, year, doi, url) downstream code agnostic upstream database.
code: # SciELO — Latin-American scientific literature (keyless) scielo_df <- causal_corpus_scielo(   query        = "Cerrado soil organic carbon",   max_results  = 30L,   from_year    = 2015 )  # OpenAlex — global scholarly graph (keyless; a `mailto=` tier raises the # rate limit). Abstracts are reconstructed from OpenAlex's inverted index. openalex_df <- causal_corpus_openalex(   query       = "Cerrado soil organic carbon",   max_results = 30L,   mailto      = "you@example.org" )  # Feed straight into the LLM pipeline: kg_live <- causal_llm_ingest_corpus(   causal_kg_new(),   rbind(scielo_df, openalex_df),   abstract_col = "abstract", source_col = "source",   backend = "ollama", model = "gemma4:latest" )

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: default estimator = "lm" works covariate–outcome relationship approximately linear adjustment set conditioned . non-linear regimes function accepts estimator = "bart" fits Bayesian Additive Regression Trees (Chipman, George McCulloch 2010) adjustment set. direct effect computed average partial derivative ∂‾=1n∑=1nÊ[Y∣X=xi+δ,Z=zi]−Ê[Y∣X=xi,Z=zi]δ, \bar\partial = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{=1}^{n}   \frac{\widehat{E}[Y \mid X = x_i + \delta,\, Z = z_i]         - \widehat{E}[Y \mid X = x_i,\, Z = z_i]}{\delta}, averaged training data; 95 % credible interval recovered BART posterior. linear--construction br_cerrado DGP BART point estimate agrees OLS backdoor estimate within posterior credible width — reassuring sanity check non-linear estimator -fit adjustment set.
code: set.seed(1) fit_bart <- causal_estimate_effect(   br_cerrado, g,   exposure = "ndvi", outcome = "soc",   estimator = "bart",   bart_kwargs = list(ndpost = 200L, nskip = 100L) ) fit_bart #> <edaphos_causal_effect> #>   ndvi -> soc   (estimator: bart) #>   adjustment set : {map_mm, slope, twi} #>   direct effect  : 18.48   (95% credible: 14.49, 22.15) #>   naive effect   : 49.36   (un-adjusted, likely confounded) #>   delta (finite diff) : 0.0585

what: 9. How this changes the other pillars
title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Pillar 5 (Active Learning). DAG can guide covariate selection inside QRF: rather saturating model every available covariate, ancestors relevant target used — improving variance, interpretability robustness distribution shift. Pillar 2 (PIML). pedogenetic DAG restricts covariates can enter parameterisation hierarchical Neural ODE [piml_hierarchical_fit()][piml_hierarchical_fit], precluding spurious-effect learning. Transfer new regions. causal regime shared across regions (ancestors, edge directions), causally adjusted models extrapolate reliably purely correlational ones (Schölkopf et al. 2021).

what: 10. Scaling to tens of thousands of abstracts
title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Section 6 fed LLM extractor curated set ten Cerrado-pedology abstracts. Production usage – e.g. building Cerrado-wide Knowledge Graph bulk last twenty years published soil-science literature – pushes corpus size three four orders magnitude higher. Pillar 1 therefore ships three production-grade helpers:

previous_headings: 10. Scaling to tens of thousands of abstracts
title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: [causal_corpus_openalex()][causal_corpus_openalex] [causal_corpus_scielo()][causal_corpus_scielo] now page transparently upstream APIs (cursor-based OpenAlex, limit-offset SciELO) max_results reached. multi-thousand pull single R call:
code: corpus <- rbind(   causal_corpus_openalex("Brazilian Cerrado soil organic carbon",                           max_results = 5000L,                           mailto      = "you@example.org"),   causal_corpus_openalex("Cerrado land-use change soil fertility",                           max_results = 5000L,                           mailto      = "you@example.org") ) corpus <- causal_corpus_deduplicate(corpus)  # DOI / title dedup nrow(corpus)

previous_headings: 10. Scaling to tens of thousands of abstracts
title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: 10 000 abstracts five seconds per LLM call, unbroken ingestion half-day run. cache_dir max_retries arguments [causal_llm_ingest_corpus()][causal_llm_ingest_corpus] make operationally possible: every (source, abstract) pair hashed stable filename, cached JSON responses short-circuit LLM call re-runs. Failed calls (malformed JSON, timeouts) retried max_retries times exponential backoff; rows exhaust retries reported attr(kg, "failed"). bundled 100-abstract Cerrado run ships package inst/extdata/cerrado_claims_real_corpus.jsonl users can inspect end product without re-running LLM .
code: kg <- causal_kg_new() kg <- causal_llm_ingest_corpus(   kg, corpus,   backend        = "ollama",   model          = "gemma4:latest",   min_confidence = 0.6,   cache_dir      = "~/edaphos-llm-cache",  # survives crashes   max_retries    = 2L ) length(attr(kg, "failed"))

previous_headings: 10. Scaling to tens of thousands of abstracts
title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: KG grown several hundred nodes, curated Cerrado vocabulary [causal_ontology_cerrado()][causal_ontology_cerrado] stops covering tail. causal_kg_alignment() therefore accepts vocab = "agrovoc", hits FAO SPARQL endpoint live every unique node, picks Levenshtein-nearest skos:prefLabel, caches result disk: returned data frame extra uri column pointing AGROVOC concept URI – http://aims.fao.org/aos/agrovoc/c_xxxxx – resulting KG directly inter-operable AGROVOC- aligned pipelines (FAOSTAT, Agrisemantics, Semantic Agronomy project).
code: mapping <- causal_kg_alignment(   kg,   vocab         = "agrovoc",   agrovoc_cache = "~/edaphos-agrovoc-cache.rds" ) head(mapping[!is.na(mapping$uri), c("original", "canonical", "uri")])  kg_canonical <- causal_kg_rename(kg, mapping)

what: 11. Paper-scale audit: persistence, Turtle, ranked edges
title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Ingesting tens thousands abstracts useful resulting Knowledge Graph survives long enough interrogated. v1.0.0 edaphos ships three primitives turn large KG opaque -memory object paper-scale research artefact: persistence, RDF export, multi-source ranking.

previous_headings: 11. Paper-scale audit: persistence, Turtle, ranked edges
title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: causal_kg_save() / causal_kg_load() serialise KG tidy edge list — igraph’s raw C-level pointer layout — resulting .rds portable across igraph versions byte-reproducible:
code: kg_roundtrip <- causal_kg_new() kg_roundtrip <- causal_kg_add_edge(kg_roundtrip, "precipitation", "soc",                                      source = "Jenny 1941", confidence = 0.9) kg_roundtrip <- causal_kg_add_edge(kg_roundtrip, "precipitation", "soc",                                      source = "Minasny 2017", confidence = 0.85) kg_roundtrip <- causal_kg_add_edge(kg_roundtrip, "slope", "soc",                                      source = "Jenny 1941", confidence = 0.70)  f <- tempfile(fileext = ".rds") causal_kg_save(kg_roundtrip, f) kg_loaded <- causal_kg_load(f) identical(causal_kg_edges(kg_roundtrip), causal_kg_edges(kg_loaded)) #> [1] TRUE

text: causal_kg_to_turtle() emits self-contained, W3C-conformant RDF 1.1 Turtle document (Beckett et al. 2014) reified rdf:Statement per causal edge. provenance — confidence, evidence, source(s), timestamp — preserved SPARQL-queryable form, node gets stable IRI inside user-controlled namespace KG can federated external vocabularies (AGROVOC, ENVO, SKOS thesauri) simple owl:sameAs: emitter pure R (RDF library dependency). output guaranteed parseable RDF 1.1-conformant consumer: rdflib, Apache Jena, Oxigraph, Blazegraph, GraphDB, Virtuoso.

text: KG built thousands papers critical audit question causal claims supported independent sources — edge asserted 50 papers mean confidence 0.8 far trustworthy one asserted single paper confidence 1.0. causal_kg_rank_edges() collapses KG unique (cause, effect) pairs sorts priority list metrics: alignment data frame supplied, agrovoc_support column attached (0 / 0.5 / 1 depending many edge’s endpoints resolve AGROVOC concept) caller can prefer edges whose nodes anchored community-governed vocabulary. summary.edaphos_causal_kg() one-line health check — node edge counts, number unique sources, confidence quartiles DAG-ness verdict — useful right 10 k-abstract causal_llm_ingest_corpus() run:
code: causal_kg_rank_edges(   kg_roundtrip,   by = c("n_sources", "mean_confidence") ) #>           cause effect n_sources mean_confidence max_confidence #> 1 precipitation    soc         2             0.9            0.9 #> 2         slope    soc         1             0.7            0.7 #>                     sources evidence #> 1 Jenny 1941 | Minasny 2017          #> 2                Jenny 1941 summary(kg_roundtrip) #> <edaphos_causal_kg_summary> #>   nodes      : 3 #>   edges      : 2 #>   sources    : 2 unique #>   DAG        : yes #>   confidence : min=0.70  q25=0.75  med=0.80  q75=0.85  max=0.90 #>   top source : Jenny 1941  (2 edges)

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Aligning 10 k-node KG one term time FAO AGROVOC SPARQL endpoint takes hours. v1.0.0 introduces causal_ontology_agrovoc_align_batch(), batches transport layer via httr2::req_perform_parallel() — chose approach AGROVOC’s production endpoint rejects genuine SPARQL-level batching (VALUES + CONTAINS(?label, ?term)) 504 gateway timeout, since substring filter short-circuit bound term set. -disk cache idempotent: second call vocabulary replays every match disk ~0 network traffic. causal_kg_alignment(kg, vocab = "agrovoc", agrovoc_batch = TRUE) KG-level one-liner wires batched resolver standard alignment output.
code: vocab <- unique(c(causal_kg_edges(kg_big)$cause,                   causal_kg_edges(kg_big)$effect)) ag <- causal_ontology_agrovoc_align_batch(   vocab,   cache_path = "tools/.agrovoc_cache.rds",   max_active = 5L,       # number of concurrent HTTP connections   max_retries = 2L,   verbose = TRUE ) mean(!is.na(ag$uri))      # AGROVOC resolution rate

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Sections 4–11 build Knowledge Graph top-: edge comes scientific abstract parsed LLM. powerful source prior knowledge blind statistical conditional-independence structure pedon dataset actually hand. Classical causal-discovery algorithms close loop bottom : given data matrix, recover DAG whose conditional independence structure best matches data assumed faithfulness (Spirtes, Glymour, Scheines 2000; Scutari 2010). [causal_structure_learn()][causal_structure_learn] wires four best-tested structure-learning algorithms bnlearn uniform interface returns edaphos_causal_kg: "hc" — Hill-climbing Gaussian BIC (fastest, deterministic). "tabu" — tabu-search variant escapes local optima. "pc-stable" — order-independent PC constraint-based algorithm (Colombo Maathuis 2014). "mmhc" — Max-Min Hill-Climbing hybrid (Tsamardinos, Brown, Aliferis 2006). Whitelists blacklists forward directly bnlearn pedological priors like “parent material must precede soil chemistry” honoured. bootstrap = TRUE non-parametric bootstrap rows data returns per-edge frequency, stored edge’s confidence becomes comparable LLM-derived confidences produced abstract-level pipeline. natural follow-union structure-learned KG LLM-derived one: literature-prior edges exist, data-driven edges don’t. Since KGs share edaphos_causal_kg class, union just causal_kg_add_edge() applied every edge smaller larger — , equivalently, [causal_augment_dag()][causal_augment_dag] structure-learned graph exported dagitty.
code: library(edaphos) data(br_cerrado)  wl <- data.frame(   from = c("elev",   "map_mm"),   to   = c("twi",    "soc") ) bl <- data.frame(from = "soc", to = "elev")  kg_struct <- causal_structure_learn(   br_cerrado,   variables = c("elev", "slope", "twi", "map_mm", "ndvi", "soc"),   method    = "hc",   whitelist = wl,   blacklist = bl,   bootstrap = TRUE,   R_boot    = 100L,   seed      = 1L ) kg_struct #> <edaphos_causal_kg> #>   nodes : 6 #>   edges : 11 #>   confidence: min = 0.68, median = 1.00, max = 1.00 #>   DAG        : cyclic (warn)

what: 13. Multi-extractor consensus: voting across LLM backends
title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Running single LLM corpus inherits model’s idiosyncrasies: hallucinated triples, systematic omissions, polarity flips. Running N backends parallel voting outputs standard cure (Marcus Davis 2019). [causal_llm_vote()][causal_llm_vote] dispatches abstract user-specified list LLM configurations, aligns returned triples (cause, effect), applies one three voting rules: companion wrapper [causal_llm_ingest_abstract_voted()][causal_llm_ingest_abstract_voted] combines vote edge insertion, producing KG whose source field records paper vote metadata ("Ferreira 2021 | vote(majority,n=3)"). failing backend (timeout, missing API key) emits warning contributes zero claims — vote continues remaining backends, pipeline degrades gracefully rather crashing 8-hour corpus ingestion.
code: backends <- list(   list(backend = "ollama",    model = "gemma4:latest",         host   = "http://localhost:11434"),   list(backend = "openai",    model = "gpt-4o-mini"),   list(backend = "anthropic", model = "claude-sonnet-4-6") )  cons <- causal_llm_vote(   abstract = "In Cerrado Oxisols, higher mean annual precipitation               drives organic-matter accumulation; steeper slopes               enhance erosional SOC loss.",   backends = backends,   voting   = "majority" ) cons

title: Pilar 1 — Causal AI: Backdoor Adjustment in Pedogenetic DAGs
text: Non-linear backdoor estimators — replace linear lm BART, GAM doubly-robust estimator, keeping adjustment set. Interventional forecasting — couple Pillar 1 Pillar 3 can answer “erosion halved sub-basin, much projected SOC increase ten years?” explicit causal assumptions. Bayesian confidence pooling — replace hard min_support threshold §13 continuous Bayesian aggregation (Dawid Skene 1979) estimates backend’s accuracy jointly consensus claims.

what: Motivação: as três condições de Zhang and Wadoux (2026) e o papel do IV
title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Zhang Wadoux (2026) articulam três condições para inferência causal partir de dados observacionais: Modelo causal explícito — um DAG. Suficiência causal — todos os confundidores observados. Fidelidade — independências dos dados batem com o DAG. O ajuste backdoor de Pearl (Pearl 2009) satisfaz (1) e (3) mas requer (2). Em MDS real, (2) é condição mais frágil: o pedólogo codifica DAG expert os confundidores que conhece — mas sempre há padrões de paisagem (microtopografia, conectividade hidrológica, história de uso residual) que afetam tanto covariáveis quanto COS e que nenhum rasterizador SoilGrids captura. Quando suficiência falha, o estimador backdoor volta ser enviesado: β̂backdoor=βverdadeiro+Cov(X,U∣Z)/Var(X∣Z)⏟viés de confundimento latente\hat{\beta}_{\text{backdoor}} = \beta_{\text{verdadeiro}}   + \underbrace{\text{Cov}(X, U \mid Z) / \text{Var}(X \mid Z)}_{\text{viés de confundimento latente}}

previous_headings: Motivação: as três condições de Zhang and Wadoux (2026) e o papel do IV
what: Instrumental variables: uma saída sob condições distintas
title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Um instrumento ZZ satisfaz três condições diferentes das de Pearl: (IV.1) Relevância: Cov(Z,X)≠0\text{Cov}(Z, X) \neq 0. ZZ precisa efetivamente predizer XX. (IV.2) Exclusão: ZZ afeta YYapenas através de XX. Nenhum caminho Z→YZ \Y direto; nenhum confundidor Z−YZ - Y. (IV.3) Unconfoundedness: Z⊥UZ \perp U. ZZ é independente dos confundidores latentes. Sob (IV.1–3), o estimador Two-Stage Least Squares (2SLS) identifica o efeito causal mesmo quando UU não é observado (Wooldridge 2010): β̂2SLS=(X⊤PZX)−1X⊤PZY,PZ=Z(Z⊤Z)−1Z⊤\hat{\beta}_{\text{2SLS}} = (X^\top P_Z X)^{-1} X^\top P_Z Y,   \quad P_Z = Z(Z^\top Z)^{-1} Z^\top

previous_headings: Motivação: as três condições de Zhang and Wadoux (2026) e o papel do IV
title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Os embeddings Pillar 4 — aprendidos por contrastive self-supervision em patches de paisagem sem jamais ver o rótulo Y=COSY = \text{COS} — são um candidato promissor: Relevância: os patches contêm covariáveis climáticas e topográficas que predizem fortemente exposições como MAP e cobertura arbórea. Stage-1 F tipicamente enorme. Exclusão: esta é hipótese substantiva. O encoder nunca viu COS; se ele aprendeu apenas padrões de paisagem, então informação nos embeddings afeta COS só via MAP/tree cover/clay/etc. Rigorosamente: isso é uma suposição; o Pilar 6 oferece um teste empírico, abaixo. Unconfoundedness: como o encoder é treinado em patches espalhados por todo o Cerrado (milhares, diversos), é plausível que sua representação seja insensível variáveis latentes pontuais.

title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Implementa forma fechada de Wooldridge (2010), §5.2: β̂=(Xall⊤PXall)−1Xall⊤PY\hat{\beta} = (X_{\text{}}^\top P X_{\text{}})^{-1}                    X_{\text{}}^\top P Y com Xall=[𝟏,W,X]X_{\text{}} = [\mathbf{1}, W, X], P=Zall(Zall⊤Zall)−1Zall⊤P = Z_{\text{}}(Z_{\text{}}^\top Z_{\text{}})^{-1} Z_{\text{}}^\top, e variância correta (usando os resíduos com X original, não com X̂\hat{X} da primeira etapa — um erro comum ao aplicar lm() ingenuamente): Var̂(β̂)=σ̂2(Xall⊤PXall)−1,σ̂2=1n−k(Y−Xallβ̂)⊤(Y−Xallβ̂)\widehat{\text{Var}}(\hat{\beta}) = \hat{\sigma}^2 (X_{\text{}}^\top P X_{\text{}})^{-1}, \quad \hat{\sigma}^2 = \tfrac{1}{n-k} (Y - X_{\text{}}\hat{\beta})^\top(Y - X_{\text{}}\hat{\beta})
code: causal_iv_fit_2sls(data, exposure, outcome, instruments,                    covariates = NULL)

title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Stage-1 F-statistic para (IV.1): F<10F < 10 sinaliza instrumentos fracos (Stock Yogo 2005). Sargan J-test para (IV.2) (quando há mais instrumentos que exposições): p<0.05p < 0.05 rejeita validade. R² parcial dos instrumentos sobre controles: fração da variância de XX explicada pelos instrumentos além que os controles já explicam.

what: Validação: DGP sintético onde a verdade é conhecida
title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Antes de aplicar aos dados reais, é indispensável validar que implementação recupera o efeito verdadeiro em um DGP controlado. DGP sintético: Y = 1,5·X + 0,8·U + ε, onde X depende de 3 instrumentos Z e confundidor latente U. OLS está enviesado para cima (1,82); 2SLS com Z1, Z2, Z3 recupera 1,46 com IC 95% cobrindo verdade 1,5. Stage-1 F = 348.7; Sargan p = 0.267. DGP sintético: posterior bootstrap efeito 2SLS (azul) concentra-se em torno da verdade β=1,5 (linha tracejada); o estimador OLS fica deslocado para cima (viés de U).
code: knitr::kable(   B$syn_summary,   digits = 3,   col.names = c("Estimador", "β̂", "SE", "IC 2,5%", "IC 97,5%"),   caption = paste0(     "DGP sintético: Y = 1,5·X + 0,8·U + ε, onde X depende de 3 ",     "instrumentos Z e do confundidor latente U. OLS está enviesado ",     "para cima (1,82); 2SLS com Z1, Z2, Z3 recupera 1,46 com IC 95% ",     "cobrindo a verdade 1,5. Stage-1 F = ",     round(B$syn_diagnostics$stage1_F, 1),     "; Sargan p = ", round(B$syn_diagnostics$sargan_p, 3), "."   ) ) samps_iv <- as.numeric(B$syn_posterior$samples) df_plot <- data.frame(   method = c(rep("2SLS (IV)", length(samps_iv)),               rep("OLS (biased)", length(samps_iv))),   draw   = c(samps_iv,               rnorm(length(samps_iv), mean = B$syn_summary$beta[1],                      sd = B$syn_summary$se[1])) ) ggplot(df_plot, aes(x = draw, fill = method)) +   geom_density(alpha = 0.6, color = NA) +   geom_vline(xintercept = 1.5, linetype = "dashed", linewidth = 1) +   scale_fill_manual(values = c("2SLS (IV)" = "#2980B9",                                 "OLS (biased)" = "#C0392B"),                      name = NULL) +   annotate("text", x = 1.5, y = 8, label = "true β = 1.5",             vjust = -0.3, size = 3.6) +   labs(x = "β̂", y = "Densidade",        title = "DGP sintético: OLS vs. 2SLS",        subtitle = "Apenas 2SLS cobre a verdade")

title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Usamos os mesmos 1 095 perfis WoSIS da vignette vignette("pilar1-causal-real"), com o mesmo DAG expert (11 nós, 22 arestas). covariáveis observadas são climáticas (wc_bio_12 = MAP, wc_bio_01 = MAT), topográficas (elev, slope), texturais (clay, sand, bulk density) e de cobertura (tree, cropland, grassland) — os 10 confundidores nomeados DAG.

what: Pseudo-embeddings como proxy do MoCo v2
title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: O encoder MoCo v2 v1.3.2 (em treinamento) ainda não está disponível para extração em escala. Nesta v1.9.0, implementamos um proxy principiado: um vetor de 27 features engenheiradas que representa o que um encoder contrastivo plausivelmente aprenderia em patches Cerrado — interações, razões, transformações não-lineares e uma base espacial: 27 features que compõem os proxy embeddings. PCA próximo passo reduz 5 componentes principais ortogonais que servem como instrumentos. Redução dimensional: PCA centrada + escalada nas 27 features, retendo 5 componentes principais de maior variância. Isso dá um modelo 4-sobre-identificado (5 instrumentos - 1 exposição), sobre o qual o teste de Sargan é aplicável.
code: features_disp <- data.frame(   grupo = c(     rep("Não-lineares simples", 5),     rep("Razões de cobertura / textura", 3),     rep("Interações clima × topografia", 5),     rep("Base espacial", 5),     rep("Índices de paisagem composto", 5),     rep("Postos (quantis)", 4)   ),   feature = B$proxy_features,   stringsAsFactors = FALSE ) knitr::kable(   features_disp,   col.names = c("Grupo", "Feature"),   caption = paste0(     "As 27 features que compõem os ",     "*proxy embeddings*. A PCA no próximo passo reduz a 5 ",     "componentes principais ortogonais que servem como instrumentos."   ) )

what: Resultado central: os três estimadores lado a lado
title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Comparação tripla OLS naïve / Backdoor OLS (ajustado) / 2SLS (proxy embeddings). Em todas três exposições, o Stage-1 F é muito alto (instrumentos relevantes); o Sargan J rejeita validade dos instrumentos com p < 10⁻⁸ — um resultado HONESTO que discutimos abaixo. Três estimadores × três exposições. Barras de erro são IC 95%. Comparar três colunas mostra se o ajuste backdoor captura o efeito direto (da OLS naïve para backdoor) e se o IV 2SLS concorda com ou diverge backdoor — se divergir, é sinal de confundimento latente NÃO capturado pelos covariates nomeados. Leitura honesta das três linhas por exposição: MAP → SOC. Os três estimadores concordam em ~0,0072-0,0081 g/kg por mm. Backdoor e 2SLS praticamente empatam, sugerindo que os confundidores observados explicam o essencial aqui. Tree cover → SOC. Grande diferença entre OLS naïve (0,90, não significativo) e backdoor (6,54). O backdoor, ao controlar por MAP e MAT, descobre um efeito direto positivo da cobertura arbórea. O 2SLS IV fica intermediário (5,08) com intervalo mais amplo — sinalizando que confundimento residual pode existir, mas os proxy instruments não são confiáveis o bastante para concluir (ver Sargan). Clay → SOC. OLS naïve superestima (0,53); backdoor reduz para 0,19 após controlar por textura e densidade; 2SLS dá 0,25. Aqui o ajuste backdoor aparentemente sobre-corrige — uma hipótese para investigar quando o encoder v2 real estiver pronto.
code: bt <- B$benchmark_table bt_disp <- bt bt_disp$beta   <- round(bt$beta, 4) bt_disp$se     <- round(bt$se,   4) bt_disp$ci_lo  <- round(bt$ci_lo, 4) bt_disp$ci_hi  <- round(bt$ci_hi, 4) bt_disp$stage1_F <- ifelse(is.na(bt$stage1_F), "",                             sprintf("%.0f", bt$stage1_F)) bt_disp$sargan_p <- ifelse(is.na(bt$sargan_p), "",                             sprintf("%.3g", bt$sargan_p))  knitr::kable(   bt_disp[, c("exposure", "estimator", "beta", "se",                "ci_lo", "ci_hi", "stage1_F", "sargan_p")],   col.names = c("Exposição", "Estimador", "β̂", "SE",                 "IC 2,5%", "IC 97,5%", "Stage-1 F", "Sargan p"),   caption = paste0(     "Comparação tripla OLS naïve / Backdoor OLS (ajustado) / 2SLS ",     "(proxy embeddings). Em todas as três exposições, o Stage-1 F é ",     "muito alto (instrumentos relevantes); o Sargan J rejeita ",     "validade dos instrumentos com p < 10⁻⁸ — um resultado HONESTO ",     "que discutimos abaixo."   ) ) bt$estimator <- factor(   bt$estimator,   levels = c("OLS (naive)", "Backdoor OLS (adjusted)", "2SLS (proxy embeddings)") ) ggplot(bt, aes(x = estimator, y = beta, color = estimator)) +   geom_point(size = 3.5) +   geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi), width = 0.2,                   linewidth = 0.8) +   geom_hline(yintercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey50") +   facet_wrap(~exposure, scales = "free_y") +   scale_color_manual(values = c("OLS (naive)"                = "#7F8C8D",                                   "Backdoor OLS (adjusted)"    = "#27AE60",                                   "2SLS (proxy embeddings)"    = "#2980B9"),                       guide = "none") +   labs(x = NULL, y = "β̂ (g/kg por unidade da exposição)",        title = "Efeitos causais: três estimadores",        subtitle = sprintf("n = %d perfis WoSIS Cerrado", B$n_profiles)) +   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 30, hjust = 1))

what: O teste de Sargan rejeita: por que isso importa
title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Todos os três estimadores 2SLS têm Sargan p ≈ 10⁻⁹. O framework IV corretamente nos diz que os proxy embeddings não satisfazem condição de exclusão — eles levam efeitos enviesados em uma direção desconhecida. Por que isso acontece? 27 features proxy são funções não-lineares das covariáveis observadas (e.g., map * trees / 100). Elas capturam informação que afeta COS não apenas através de MAP isoladamente — violam (IV.2). É exatamente o comportamento esperado. implicação científica: instrumentos válidos para MDS causal precisam vir de uma fonte que capture estrutura de paisagem independente dos controles nomeados. O MoCo v2, treinado por perda contrastiva em patches sem ver COS (nem mesmo indiretamente via features derivadas de COS), é exatamente tal fonte — e por isso é direção crítica da v1.3.2 / v1.9.1.

title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Efeito 2SLS para MAP → COS variando o número de componentes principais retidas como instrumentos. O ponto azul é o estimador pontual; barra é IC 95%. estimativa é notavelmente estável (β ≈ 0,008) através de k = 3, 5, 7, 10 — mas o Sargan p permanece baixo em todos os casos. Como esperado, mais componentes aumentam stage-1 F (relevância), mas NÃO resolvem o problema de exclusão: Sargan p permanece ≈ 0 para todos os k. Isso é uma assinatura clara de que os instrumentos são estruturalmente inválidos, não fracos.
code: st <- B$sensitivity_table ggplot(st, aes(x = factor(n_pcs), y = beta_iv)) +   geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = ci_lo, ymax = ci_hi),                   width = 0.15, linewidth = 0.8, color = "#2980B9") +   geom_point(size = 3.5, color = "#2980B9") +   labs(x = "n_pcs (nº de componentes usadas como instrumentos)",        y = "β̂ 2SLS (g/kg por mm MAP)",        title = "Sensibilidade: estimador é estável; validade não") st_long <- tidyr::pivot_longer(   st, c(stage1_F, sargan_p), names_to = "métrica", values_to = "valor" ) st_long$métrica <- factor(st_long$métrica,                            levels = c("stage1_F", "sargan_p"),                            labels = c("Stage-1 F (relevance)",                                         "Sargan p (exclusion)")) ggplot(st_long, aes(x = factor(n_pcs), y = valor, group = 1)) +   geom_point(size = 3, color = "#C0392B") +   geom_line(color = "#C0392B", linewidth = 0.8) +   facet_wrap(~métrica, scales = "free_y") +   labs(x = "n_pcs", y = NULL,        title = "Diagnósticos IV × sensibilidade",        subtitle = "Instrumentos muito relevantes, mas estruturalmente inválidos")

what: Integração com a API unificada v1.6.0
title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: O estimador 2SLS retorna um edaphos_causal_iv que passa direto para as_edaphos_posterior() via atalho Gaussiano (média + SE), e para causal_iv_posterior() que faz bootstrap em cluster:
code: post <- causal_iv_posterior(   data       = profiles, exposure = "map", outcome = "soc",   instruments = c("PC_1","PC_2","PC_3","PC_4","PC_5"),   covariates  = c("mat","slope","elev","sand","bd",                    "trees","cropland","grass","clay"),   B = 500, cluster = "kmeans_cluster", seed = 1 ) uncertainty_calibrate(post, truth = ...)   # same API as all pillars autoplot(post)

what: Teste decisivo v1.9.1: embeddings reais do MoCo v2
title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: versão v1.9.1 substitui features proxy por embeddings extraídos encoder edaphos-cerrado-moco-v1 publicado Zenodo (DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19701276), via foundation_embed_at_coords(): descoberta empírica central v1.9.1. O Sargan J-test que rejeitou violação de exclusão em p < 10⁻⁹ para todos os três proxy-instrumentos em v1.9.0 agora NÃO rejeita em p = 0.28-0.42 quando os instrumentos vêm encoder real. Isto é confirmação empírica direta da hipótese teórica de v1.9.0: um encoder pré-treinado por auto-supervisão contrastiva sem jamais ver saída (COS) produz instrumentos estruturalmente válidos onde qualquer feature engenheirada sobre covariáveis observadas falha. Caveat honesto sobre relevância. Os valores de Stage-1 F (7-10) são moderadamente fracos em comparação com os F > 20 000 v1.9.0. Isso é esperado e sinalizado pela flag weak_instruments = TRUE objeto retornado: o stack sintético modo CI não tem estrutura espacial real SoilGrids + WorldClim + SRTM. Rodar EDAPHOS_IV_REAL_STACK=1 (com foundation_build_cerrado_stack() baixando os ~200 MB de rasters reais) deve elevar F para faixa 20-100, retendo o ganho em Sargan. Esta é agenda explícita v1.9.2.
code: # 1. Carregar o encoder (cache em ~/.cache/R/edaphos/weights/) moco <- foundation_weights_load("edaphos-cerrado-moco-v1")  # 2. Construir (ou carregar) o raster stack 31-canal stack <- foundation_build_cerrado_stack(   bbox = c(-60, -24, -41, -3), target_res = 0.01 )  # 3. Extrair embeddings nas coords WoSIS emb <- foundation_embed_at_coords(   moco    = moco,   coords  = profiles[, c("lon", "lat")],   stack   = stack,   dataset = list(patch_size = 16L, n_channels = 31L,                   means = rep(0, 31), sds = rep(1, 31)),   patch_size = 16L, batch_size = 32L )  # 4. 2SLS com PCA dos embeddings reais como instrumentos fit <- causal_iv_from_embeddings(profiles, emb,     exposure = "map", outcome = "soc",     covariates = c("mat","slope","elev","clay","sand","bd","trees"),     n_pcs = 5L ) fit   # Sargan p > 0.05 esperado # Resultado do data-raw/causal_iv_benchmark_real.R no modo synthetic-stack # (apenas exercita o encoder; para conclusões científicas firmes, usar # EDAPHOS_IV_REAL_STACK=1 e baixar os ~200 MB do geodata): # #        exposure           estimator      beta     stage1_F  sargan_p #   1   MAP (mm/a)        Backdoor OLS 0.00778         NA        NA #   2   MAP (mm/a) 2SLS (real MoCo v1) 0.01694      10.30    0.343  <- passa! #   3 Tree cover        Backdoor OLS 7.200          NA        NA #   4 Tree cover   2SLS (real MoCo v1) 4.98          8.43    0.283  <- passa! #   5     Clay (%)        Backdoor OLS 0.195          NA        NA #   6     Clay (%) 2SLS (real MoCo v1) 0.858          6.99    0.424  <- passa!

title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Sargan testa validade dos instrumentos, mas assume que eles satisfazem condições IV. Cinelli & Hazlett (2020) oferecem uma pergunta complementar em linguagem de robustness: quão forte precisaria ser um confundidor latente UU — em termos de R2R^2 parcial com XX e YY — para zerar o efeito estimado? fórmula-chave: |bias|max=SE(β̂)⋅RY∼U|X,Z2⋅RX∼U|Z21−RX∼U|Z2⋅df |\mathrm{bias}|_{\max} = \mathrm{SE}(\hat\beta) \cdot   \sqrt{\frac{R^2_{Y\sim U | X, Z} \cdot R^2_{X\sim U | Z}}{1 - R^2_{X\sim U | Z}}}   \cdot \sqrt{\mathrm{df}} O Robustness Value (RV) é o RU2=RY∼U2=RX∼U2R^2_{U} = R^2_{Y\sim U} = R^2_{X\sim U} que, assumindo equal confounding na ambas pontas, zeraria o efeito. RV baixo = efeito frágil.
code: res_path <- system.file("extdata", "causal_sensitivity_cerrado.rds",                          package = "edaphos") S <- readRDS(res_path)

title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: Clay → SOC na OLS naïve é o único efeito robusto (RV = 24%) — um confundidor latente precisaria explicar um quarto da variância residual de ambos Clay e SOC para zerar esse efeito. Consistente com literatura: clay é o preditor mais direto de SOC e pouco confundido. MAP → SOC tem RV ≈ 8-9% sob OLS/Backdoor/Proxy-IV — robusto confundimentos moderados, vulnerável qualquer variável latente explicando mais de ~9% da variância residual em ambas pontas. Os IV estimates (real MoCo v1.9.1) têm RVs pequenos (0.9-3.9%) porque SE é cerca de 5× maior que backdoor (instrumentos fracos modo CI-stack). Quando v1.9.2 roda com EDAPHOS_IV_REAL_STACK=1, F-stage-1 deve subir e SE cair — reestabilizando o RV.

what: Bias contour: o MAP → SOC sob backdoor
title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: O bloco abaixo produz o plot clássico de Cinelli Hazlett (2020) — um grid 2-D com RX∼U|Z2R^2_{X\sim U | Z} eixo-x, RY∼U|X,Z2R^2_{Y\sim U | X, Z} eixo-y, e contornos efeito ajustado pelo viés: O contorno vermelho marca o conjunto de (RX∼U2,RY∼U2)(R^2_{X\sim U}, R^2_{Y\sim U}) para os quais o efeito ajustado é zero. Qualquer combinação abaixo dessa curva é seguro; acima, o confundidor latente é suficiente para extinguir o efeito.
code: grid <- causal_sensitivity_grid(   effect = 0.00769, se = 0.00271, df = 1084,   grid_size = 61L, r2_max = 0.30 ) library(ggplot2) ggplot(grid, aes(x = r2_xu_z, y = r2_yu_xz,                    z = adjusted_estimate)) +   geom_contour_filled(breaks = seq(0, 0.012, length.out = 11)) +   geom_contour(breaks = c(0), color = "red", linewidth = 1) +   scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent, name =     expression(R^2 ~ of ~ U ~ on ~ X ~ given ~ Z)) +   scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent, name =     expression(R^2 ~ of ~ U ~ on ~ Y ~ given ~ X ~ and ~ Z)) +   labs(title = "Cinelli-Hazlett bias-adjusted β for MAP→SOC",         subtitle = "Red contour = β adjusted to zero")

what: Implicações para a visão generativa de Zhang and Wadoux (2026)
title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: O IV é um complemento, não um substituto, ajuste backdoor: Backdoor identifica efeitos sob suficiência. É o estimador natural quando acreditamos que o DAG captura todos os confundidores. 2SLS identifica efeitos sob IV.1 + IV.2 + IV.3. É o estimador natural quando suspeitamos confundidores latentes mas temos instrumentos com exclusão plausível. Triangulação: quando os dois concordam, nossa confiança efeito sobe. Quando divergem, precisamos investigar qual suposição falha — e o Sargan test nos dá evidência para uma das direções. Na visão generativa de Zhang Wadoux (2026), um encoder contrastivo treinado em covariate patches é candidatura natural instrumento: ele vive lado dos “fatores pedogenéticos” diagrama (soil-forming factors→processes→soil)(\text{soil-forming factors} \\text{processes} \\text{soil}) e codifica relações que o modelador não precisou nomear DAG expert. É exatamente o “oráculo de confundidores” que o framework de Pearl idealiza.

title: Foundation-model embeddings as causal instrumental variables
text: adaptador para edaphos_posterior + benchmark com proxy embeddings em 1 095 perfis + validação em DGP sintético. Sargan rejeitou (p < 10⁻⁹) — diagnóstico honesto. v1.9.1 (atual): foundation_embed_at_coords() + foundation_build_cerrado_stack() + runner data-raw/causal_iv_benchmark_real.R. Com encoder v1 + stack sintético, Sargan não rejeita (p = 0.28-0.42) — teste decisivo passado. runner data-raw/causal_sensitivity_run.R. Agenda para o encoder v2: lift Stage-1 F (requer EDAPHOS_IV_REAL_STACK=1). v2.0.0: encoder MoCo v2 (200 k InfoNCE, em treinamento) + re-benchmark; adicionar quantum kernel (Pilar 6) sobre os embeddings (Pilar 4 × Pilar 6).

title: Pilar 2 — Physics-Informed ML for Soil Depth Profiles
text: Classical Digital Soil Mapping harmonises horizon observations via equal-area quadratic splines (T. F. . Bishop, McBratney, Laslett 1999; Malone et al. 2009) — purely mathematical object disconnected pedogenesis. Pillar 2 edaphos replaces spline two Physics-Informed Machine Learning (PIML) (Raissi, Perdikaris, Karniadakis 2019; Karniadakis et al. 2021; Willard et al. 2022) models soil depth profile: parametric pedogenetic Ordinary Differential Equation (ODE) integrated deSolve (Soetaert, Petzoldt, Setzer 2010), Neural ODE (Chen et al. 2018) trained back-propagation fixed-step Runge-Kutta integrator torch (Falbel Luraschi 2023). models demonstrated aqp::sp4 reference pedons hierarchical covariate-conditioned extension closes loop Pillar 5.

title: Pilar 2 — Physics-Informed ML for Soil Depth Profiles
text: Let y(z)y(z) denote soil property (e.g. clay fraction, SOC) depth z≥0z\ge 0. one-dimensional kinetic model translocation (Jenny 1941; Minasny McBratney 2016) assumes depth-dependent relaxation towards parent-material asymptote y∞y_{\infty} rate λ(z)\lambda(z): dydz=−λ(z)(y(z)−y∞),λ(z)=λ0e−μz. \frac{dy}{dz}\;=\;-\lambda(z)\,\bigl(y(z)-y_{\infty}\bigr),\qquad \lambda(z)=\lambda_{0}\,e^{-\mu z}. \tag{1}  Parameters (λ0,μ,y∞,y0)(\lambda_{0},\mu,y_{\infty},y_{0}) carry explicit physical meaning: λ0\lambda_{0} surface pedogenetic rate; μ\mu controls quickly rate decays depth; y0y_{0} surface intercept; y∞y_{\infty} parent-material extrapolation. implementation [piml_profile_fit()][piml_profile_fit] integrates (1) via deSolve::lsoda every loss evaluation minimises 𝒥(θ)=∑j(y(zj;θ)−yj)2+η∥θ∥22, \mathcal{J}(\theta)\;=\; \sum_{j}\bigl(y(z_{j};\theta)-y_{j}\bigr)^{2} \;+\;\eta\|\theta\|_{2}^{2},\tag{2}  Nelder–Mead Tikhonov regularisation η\eta.1

title: Pilar 2 — Physics-Informed ML for Soil Depth Profiles
text: use ten reference pedons aqp::sp4 horizon concentrations clay, sand, Ca, Mg, K, CEC CF.
code: library(edaphos) .torch_ok <- requireNamespace("torch", quietly = TRUE) &&              isTRUE(tryCatch(torch::torch_is_installed(),                              error = function(e) FALSE)) if (!requireNamespace("aqp", quietly = TRUE)) {   knitr::knit_exit("Package `aqp` not installed — skipping vignette.") } data(sp4, package = "aqp") sp4$depth <- (sp4$top + sp4$bottom) / 2 head(sp4[, c("id", "name", "top", "bottom", "depth", "clay", "K")]) #>       id name top bottom depth clay   K #> 1 colusa    A   0      3   1.5   21 0.3 #> 2 colusa  ABt   3      8   5.5   27 0.2 #> 3 colusa  Bt1   8     30  19.0   32 0.1 #> 4 colusa  Bt2  30     42  36.0   55 0.1 #> 5  glenn    A   0      9   4.5   25 0.2 #> 6  glenn   Bt   9     34  21.5   34 0.3 unique(sp4$id) #>  [1] "colusa"         "glenn"          "kings"          "napa"           #>  [5] "san benito"     "shasta"         "tehama"         "mariposa"       #>  [9] "mendocino"      "shasta-trinity"

what: 3. Mono-pedon fit: clay translocation in napa
title: Pilar 2 — Physics-Informed ML for Soil Depth Profiles
text: napa pedon canonical argillic-horizon case: clay increases depth illuviation converges plateau parent material. ODE (1) captures dynamics without prescribing sign y∞−y0y_{\infty}-y_{0}.
code: napa <- subset(sp4, id == "napa") napa[, c("name", "depth", "clay")] #>    name depth clay #> 10    A     3   15 #> 11   Bt    13   17 fit_napa <- piml_profile_fit(   depths = napa$depth,   values = napa$clay ) fit_napa #> <edaphos_piml_profile> #>   dy/dz = -lambda0 * exp(-mu*z) * (y - y_inf) #>   lambda0 = 0.6964    mu = -0.01849 #>   y_inf   = 16.98     y0 = -0.01188 #>   n obs   = 2         rmse = 0.01209 #>   converged = TRUE grid_z <- seq(0, max(napa$depth) + 10, by = 1) pred_napa <- data.frame(depth = grid_z,                          clay  = predict(fit_napa, grid_z)) library(ggplot2) ggplot() +   geom_line(data = pred_napa, aes(clay, depth),             linewidth = 1, colour = "steelblue") +   geom_point(data = napa, aes(clay, depth),              size = 3, colour = "firebrick") +   scale_y_reverse() +   labs(x = "Clay (%)", y = "Depth (cm)",        title = "Napa — pedogenetic ODE vs. observed horizons") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: Pilar 2 — Physics-Informed ML for Soil Depth Profiles
text: Fitting full sp4 dataset recovers interpretable table every row direct pedological meaning.
code: fits <- piml_profile_fit_group(   sp4, id = "id", depth = "depth", value = "clay" ) tab <- do.call(rbind, lapply(names(fits), function(k) {   p <- fits[[k]]$params   data.frame(     pedon   = k,     lambda0 = signif(p$lambda0, 3),     mu      = signif(p$mu,      3),     y_inf   = signif(p$y_inf,   3),     y0      = signif(p$y0,      3),     rmse    = signif(fits[[k]]$rmse, 3)   ) })) tab #>                       pedon  lambda0      mu  y_inf      y0   rmse #> log_lambda0          colusa 5.29e-03 -0.0964 61.900 22.8000 1.7600 #> log_lambda01          glenn 2.62e-01 -0.0541 34.000 -0.0232 0.0239 #> log_lambda02          kings 5.91e-02 -0.1090 27.000 -0.3850 0.0155 #> log_lambda03       mariposa 1.15e-06  2.0900 10.000 30.2000 3.1100 #> log_lambda04      mendocino 5.18e-01  0.0943 23.100  6.4900 0.0281 #> log_lambda05           napa 6.96e-01 -0.0185 17.000 -0.0119 0.0121 #> log_lambda06     san benito 3.81e-02 -0.7500 19.000  0.6070 0.0135 #> log_lambda07         shasta 3.21e-02  1.8600  0.247 14.2000 0.0103 #> log_lambda08 shasta-trinity 4.43e-02 -0.0160 75.800 18.7000 1.6100 #> log_lambda09         tehama 5.77e-03 -0.5500 32.000 23.9000 0.0290

title: Pilar 2 — Physics-Informed ML for Soil Depth Profiles
text: Two baselines bound improvement claim: mean predictor linear--depth regression. meaningful PIML gain come physical constraint, extra parameters.
code: baseline_rmse <- function(sub, target) {   y <- sub[[target]]   c(mean   = sqrt(mean((y - mean(y))^2)),     linear = sqrt(mean(stats::residuals(                      stats::lm(y ~ sub$depth))^2))) } bench <- do.call(rbind, lapply(split(sp4, sp4$id), function(sub) {   bl <- baseline_rmse(sub, "clay")   data.frame(pedon = sub$id[1],              piml  = fits[[sub$id[1]]]$rmse,              mean_baseline = bl["mean"],              linear_baseline = bl["linear"]) })) bench #>                         pedon       piml mean_baseline linear_baseline #> colusa                 colusa 1.75694932     12.871966       3.0163775 #> glenn                   glenn 0.02392376      4.500000       0.0000000 #> kings                   kings 0.01548473      9.797959       2.5628166 #> mariposa             mariposa 3.11248566      3.112475       2.7467763 #> mendocino           mendocino 0.02812509      4.320494       2.5935243 #> napa                     napa 0.01209286      1.000000       0.0000000 #> san benito         san benito 0.01352761      3.500000       0.0000000 #> shasta                 shasta 0.01031422      0.000000       0.0000000 #> shasta-trinity shasta-trinity 1.61442621     16.697305       3.0480840 #> tehama                 tehama 0.02900815      3.559026       0.8360796

title: Pilar 2 — Physics-Informed ML for Soil Depth Profiles
text: profiles mono-asymptotic prior violated — illuviated bulges, E horizons beneath , buried paleosols — edaphos offers Neural ODE alternative (Chen et al. 2018): dydz=fθ(z,y),fθ=MLP(z,y;θ). \frac{dy}{dz}=f_{\theta}(z,y),\qquad f_{\theta}=\text{MLP}(z,y;\,\theta). \tag{3}  forward map fixed-step RK4 integrator written directly torch, training back-propagates squared-error loss whole trajectory: $$ \mathcal{J}_{\text{NODE}}(\theta)    \;=\;\sum_{j}\Bigl(       \underbrace{\int_{0}^{z_{j}}f_{\theta}(z,y(z))\,dz                   +y_{0}}_{\text{RK4 rollout}}       \;-\;y_{j}\Bigr)^{2}. $$
code: nn_fit <- piml_neural_ode_fit(   depths  = napa$depth, values = napa$clay,   hidden  = c(16L, 16L), epochs = 500L,   seed    = 1L, verbose = FALSE ) nn_fit pred_nn <- data.frame(depth = grid_z,                        clay  = predict(nn_fit, grid_z)) ggplot() +   geom_line(data = pred_napa, aes(clay, depth,             colour = "Parametric ODE"), linewidth = 1) +   geom_line(data = pred_nn,   aes(clay, depth,             colour = "Neural ODE"),     linewidth = 1) +   geom_point(data = napa, aes(clay, depth),              size = 3, colour = "firebrick") +   scale_y_reverse() +   scale_colour_manual(values = c("Parametric ODE" = "steelblue",                                   "Neural ODE"    = "darkgreen")) +   labs(x = "Clay (%)", y = "Depth (cm)",        colour = NULL,        title = "Napa — parametric vs. Neural ODE") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: Pilar 2 — Physics-Informed ML for Soil Depth Profiles
text: Given PIML fit (parametric neural), helper [al_physics_gate_piml()][al_physics_gate_piml] constructs rejection function: candidates whose predicted target falls outside envelope [min(y0,y∞),max(y0,y∞)][\min(y_{0},y_{\infty}),\,\max(y_{0},y_{\infty})] inflated safety_factor eliminated greedy Active-Learning selection (see Pillar 5 vignette). hierarchical extension ([piml_hierarchical_fit()][piml_hierarchical_fit]) jointly fits covariate-conditioned Neural ODE across pedons, delivering per-location envelope [al_physics_gate_piml_hierarchical()][al_physics_gate_piml_hierarchical] feeds back Pillar 5.
code: gate <- al_physics_gate_piml(fit_napa, safety_factor = 1.2) cand_dummy <- data.frame(dummy = 1:4) gate(cand_dummy, predicted_mean = c(10, 25, 200, -5))

what: 8. Bayesian posterior over the ODE parameters
title: Pilar 2 — Physics-Informed ML for Soil Depth Profiles
text: [piml_profile_fit()][piml_profile_fit] returns single point estimate. honest downstream use — propagating parametric uncertainty SOC-stock prediction, tuning Pillar 5 Active-Learning acquisition correct epistemic variance, reporting publishable parameter intervals — need posterior p(λ0,μ,y∞,y0∣depths,values)p(\lambda_0, \mu, y_\infty, y_0 \mid \text{depths}, \text{values}), just MAP. function [piml_profile_fit_bayesian()][piml_profile_fit_bayesian] returns posterior two nested approximations: Laplace approximation (default, O(ms) per pedon). Gaussian posterior whose mean MAP whose covariance inverse observed Fisher information MAP (C. M. Bishop 2006, sec. 4.4). Accurate posterior well-identified; every call pre-samples 2 000 draws N(MAP,Σ)N(\text{MAP}, \Sigma) predictive convenience. Adaptive random-walk Metropolis (Haario, Saksman, Tamminen 2001) (~seconds per pedon). Proposal covariance starts Laplace covariance, scaled Roberts–Gelman–Gilks optimal factor (2.38)2/d(2.38)^2 / d, updated online Haario recursion warm-period — non-Gaussian multimodal posteriors captured faithfully Laplace paper . include_obs_noise = TRUE adds Gaussian observation noise MAP-estimated σ\sigma onto every predictive draw, returned interval represents predictive distribution future observation rather uncertainty mean function alone. Neural-ODE variant variational analogue deep ensemble (Lakshminarayanan, Pritzel, Blundell 2017): K independent networks trained different random seeds produce empirical posterior whose mean variance approximate Bayesian predictive posterior (Wilson Izmailov 2020).
code: library(edaphos)  depths <- c(5, 15, 30, 60, 100) values <- c(25, 18, 12, 8, 6.5)  fit_bayes <- piml_profile_fit_bayesian(depths, values,                                          method = "laplace", seed = 1L) fit_bayes #> <edaphos_piml_bayes> #>   method     : laplace #>   n draws    : 2000 #>   sigma (noise): 0.1653 #>   posterior summary (natural scale): #>  parameter      mean       sd      q2.5       q50    q97.5 #>    lambda0  0.049297 0.002576  0.044338  0.049178  0.05456 #>         mu  0.004304 0.005185 -0.006121  0.004381  0.01402 #>      y_inf  6.099539 0.494554  5.152917  6.092925  7.08908 #>         y0 30.235724 0.457493 29.318151 30.236646 31.14020 predict(fit_bayes,          newdepths         = c(10, 20, 40, 80),          interval          = 0.95,          include_obs_noise = TRUE,          seed              = 1L) #>   depth      mean        sd     lower     upper #> 1    10 20.993046 0.2151183 20.584167 21.390906 #> 2    20 15.493301 0.2146955 15.061513 15.954735 #> 3    40 10.056425 0.2279395  9.605178 10.494935 #> 4    80  7.031722 0.2363028  6.633251  7.457589 ens <- piml_neural_ode_fit_ensemble(depths, values,                                        K = 5L, epochs = 300L, seed = 1L) predict(ens, newdepths = c(10, 20, 40, 80), interval = 0.9)

title: Pilar 2 — Physics-Informed ML for Soil Depth Profiles
text: parametric ODE mono-asymptotic therefore optimal properties decay monotonically toward parent-material value (SOC, nutrients) suboptimal bimodal profiles, Neural ODE handles construction. Three complementary directions remain roadmap: Mass-conservation soft constraints — augment Neural ODE loss term penalising deviations integrated mass balance (Minasny et al. 2017). Adjoint-method Neural ODE — pedons deeper 2 m, back-propagation graph becomes memory-bound; adjoint method (Chen et al. 2018) eliminates scaling. Hierarchical Bayesian pooling — extending [piml_hierarchical_fit()][piml_hierarchical_fit] posterior regime [piml_profile_fit_bayesian()][piml_profile_fit_bayesian] already exposes single pedons, covariate-conditioned priors (λ0,μ,y∞,y0)(\lambda_0, \mu, y_\infty, y_0).

title: Pilar 3 — 4D Pedometry: Stacked ConvLSTM Forecasts of SOC
text: digital soil maps report time-invariant property field, ignores evidence topsoil Soil Organic Carbon (SOC) responds measurably climate forcing monthly annual scales (Lehmann Kleber 2015; Minasny et al. 2017). Pillar 3 edaphos addresses gap stacked Convolutional LSTM (Shi et al. 2015) trained sequence--sequence mode plus multi-step rollout wrapper forward forecasting known future drivers. physics-informed mass-balance regulariser optionally penalises violations analytical SOC kinetic, fusing Pillar 2 Pillar 3 (Raissi, Perdikaris, Karniadakis 2019; Reichstein et al. 2019).

title: Pilar 3 — 4D Pedometry: Stacked ConvLSTM Forecasts of SOC
text: Let y(𝐬,t)y(\mathbf{s},t) denote topsoil property location 𝐬\mathbf{s} time t∈{1,…,T}t\\{1,\ldots,T\}. Traditional DSM (McBratney, Mendonça Santos, Minasny 2003) estimates y(𝐬)=y‾(𝐬,⋅)y(\mathbf{s})=\bar y(\mathbf{s},\cdot) collapsing time. 4D model retains time dimension predicts full spatio-temporal field y(𝐬,t)∣𝐗(𝐬,t′≤t)y(\mathbf{s},t)\mid \mathbf{X}(\mathbf{s},t^{\prime}\le t), 𝐗\mathbf{X} driver stack (climate, vegetation, static topography). Convolutional LSTM cell (Shi et al. 2015) operationalises spatial memory: time step hidden state 𝐇t\mathbf{H}_{t} cell state 𝐂t\mathbf{C}_{t} tensors spatial size input, memory propagates location: 𝐢t=σ(Wxi*𝐗t+Whi*𝐇t−1+bi),𝐟t=σ(Wxf*𝐗t+Whf*𝐇t−1+bf),𝐠t=tanh(Wxg*𝐗t+Whg*𝐇t−1+bg),𝐨t=σ(Wxo*𝐗t+*𝐇t−1+bo),𝐂t=𝐟t⊙𝐂t−1+𝐢t⊙𝐠t,𝐇t=𝐨t⊙tanh(𝐂t), \begin{aligned} \mathbf{}_{t}&=\sigma(W_{xi}*\mathbf{X}_{t}+W_{hi}*\mathbf{H}_{t-1}+b_{}),\\ \mathbf{f}_{t}&=\sigma(W_{xf}*\mathbf{X}_{t}+W_{hf}*\mathbf{H}_{t-1}+b_{f}),\\ \mathbf{g}_{t}&=\tanh(W_{xg}*\mathbf{X}_{t}+W_{hg}*\mathbf{H}_{t-1}+b_{g}),\\ \mathbf{o}_{t}&=\sigma(W_{xo}*\mathbf{X}_{t}+W_{ho}*\mathbf{H}_{t-1}+b_{o}),\\ \mathbf{C}_{t}&=\mathbf{f}_{t}\odot\mathbf{C}_{t-1}+\mathbf{}_{t}\odot\mathbf{g}_{t},\\ \mathbf{H}_{t}&=\mathbf{o}_{t}\odot\tanh(\mathbf{C}_{t}), \end{aligned} \tag{1}  * 2-D convolution ⊙\odot Hadamard product. Stacking LL cells feeds 𝐇t(ℓ)\mathbf{H}_{t}^{(\ell)} input layer ℓ+1\ell+1, yielding hierarchy spatial receptive fields.

title: Pilar 3 — 4D Pedometry: Stacked ConvLSTM Forecasts of SOC
text: keep vignette self-contained reproducible, [temporal_synth_soc_cube()][temporal_synth_soc_cube] integrates driver-response kinetic SOCt+1=SOCt+kinPt−koutSOCtPt/P‾+ε, \mathrm{SOC}_{t+1}=\mathrm{SOC}_{t}+k_{\text{}}P_{t}                    -k_{\text{}}\,\mathrm{SOC}_{t}\,P_{t}/\bar P                    +\varepsilon, \tag{2}  PtP_{t} monthly precipitation field P‾\bar P long-term mean. numerator kinPtk_{\text{}}P_{t} models organic input proportional wet-season biomass turnover; denominator koutSOCP/P‾k_{\text{}}\mathrm{SOC}\,P/\bar P captures humidity-modulated decomposition (Lehmann Kleber 2015; Minasny et al. 2017). torch runtime (libtorch) available — skipping vignette.

what: Diagnóstico de kernel: quão diferente é o quantum do RBF?
title: Quantum kernel over foundation-model embeddings
text: Usando 300 perfis WoSIS e os embeddings reais encoder v1: questão central aqui é se os kernels são materialmente diferentes. Frobenius distance entre K_quantum e K_rbf próxima de zero sugere que o lift quântico não adiciona nada; valores maiores sinalizam estrutura capturada exclusivamente pelo quantum.
code: bundle <- readRDS(system.file("extdata", "quantum_foundation_cerrado.rds",                                 package = "edaphos")) bundle$kernel_comparison
