text: Rodando data-raw/quantum_foundation_benchmark.R:
code: bundle$cv_summary #>  method                          rmse_mean  rmse_sd  mae_mean  r2_mean  n_folds #>  Quantum KRR on foundation PCs   14.3        5.9      8.7      0.00     5 #>  Quantum KRR on raw covariates   14.2        5.9      8.4      0.00     5 #>  RBF-KRR on foundation PCs       14.8        4.6      9.8      0.00     5 #>  ranger (raw covariates)         14.3        4.0      9.0      0.08     5

title: Quantum kernel over foundation-model embeddings
text: Os quatro métodos empatam em RMSE (~14 g/kg). Apenas o ranger atinge R2>0R^2 > 0 (consistente com o estudo case-cerrado-end--end v1.3.1 onde o QRF lidera com RMSE ≈13,5\approx 13{,}5 g/kg sobre 1095 perfis reais). três regressões por kernel degeneram para R² = 0 — o mínimo aplicável — sinalizando que elas prevêem essencialmente média sobre partição espacial de teste. Por que isso? Três hipóteses alternativas, cada uma falseável: O stack sintético não carrega estrutura espacial real — o encoder vê ruído gaussiano, produz embeddings sem sinal. Quando rodamos com EDAPHOS_IV_REAL_STACK=1 (v1.9.3), isto se resolve. O encoder v1 foi sub-treinado (20 k InfoNCE steps, ~10% orçamento MoCo v2 canônico). O v2 (200 k steps, em treinamento) deve produzir representações mais ricas — teste decisivo para o Pilar 4 × Pilar 6. 6 qubits são pouco para capturar não-linearidade problema. Aumentar para 8-10 qubits (ainda classicamente simulável) + mais reps na camada de entangling ZZFeatureMap é uma direção natural — mas o custo computacional sobe como O(N2⋅4n)O(N^2 \cdot 4^n).

what: O que isso significa como contribuição científica
title: Quantum kernel over foundation-model embeddings
text: O valor v2.0.0 não é bater os baselines (tentamos e não batemos). É: Construir infraestrutura que compõe Pilar 4 com Pilar 6 de modo completo e reproduzível — qf_embed_reduce(), qf_kernel_compare(), qf_krr_fit(), qf_krr_benchmark(), todas exportadas e testadas. Formalizar hipótese — fusão quantum-foundation é um candidato natural arcabouço generativo de Zhang Wadoux (2026) e de (SchuldKilloran2019?), mas requer ou um encoder mais rico ou um stack mais real. Deixar o teste decisivo pronto — quando o encoder v2 e os dados reais de geodata estiverem disponíveis, basta re-rodar data-raw/quantum_foundation_benchmark.R para obter uma comparação equitativa.

title: Quantum kernel over foundation-model embeddings
text: v2.0.0 (atual): infraestrutura Pilar 4 × Pilar 6 completa; kernel comparison; 4-way CV benchmark empata em ~14 g/kg RMSE sobre 1 095 perfis com stack sintético. v2.0.1: re-rodar com encoder v2 (200 k InfoNCE steps, em treinamento). Se Quantum--foundation superar todos os baselines, hipótese Havlíček et al. (2019) é confirmada domínio de MDS. v2.0.2: re-rodar com raster stack real (EDAPHOS_IV_REAL_STACK=1). Se o ganho aparece aqui mas não com o stack sintético, isso responde se limitação está encoder ou stack. v2.0.3: submissão ao CRAN + paper “Quantum kernels foundation-model embeddings Digital Soil Mapping” (se resultados confirmarem ganho).

title: Pilar 4 — Contrastive Raster Embeddings as Active-Learning Covariates
text: Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) emerged dominant representation-learning paradigm computer vision (Chen et al. 2020; et al. 2020; Oord, Li, Vinyals 2018) increasingly applied remote sensing (Jean et al. 2019; Reichstein et al. 2019). Pillar 4 edaphos ships minimal SimCLR-style pipeline (Chen et al. 2020) pre-trains small raster encoder unlabelled covariate patches; resulting per-pixel embeddings used additional covariates inside Pillar 5 Active Learning loop br_cerrado. report head--head comparison identical AL policy without learned features.

title: Pilar 4 — Contrastive Raster Embeddings as Active-Learning Covariates
text: Given mini-batch {𝐱i}=1B\{\mathbf{x}_{}\}_{=1}^{B} raster patches, SimCLR draws two independent stochastic augmentations 𝐱̃(1),𝐱̃(2)\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{}^{(1)},\tilde{\mathbf{x}}_{}^{(2)} patch, encodes shared CNN fθf_{\theta} projection head gϕg_{\phi}, minimises normalised temperature-scaled cross-entropy (NT-Xent) loss (Chen et al. 2020): ℒNT-Xent=−12B∑=1B∑v∈{1,2}logexp(sim(𝐳i(v),𝐳i(v′))/τ)∑k≠(,v)exp(sim(𝐳i(v),𝐳k)/τ), \mathcal{L}_{\text{NT-Xent}}    \;=\;-\frac{1}{2B}          \sum_{=1}^{B}\sum_{v\\{1,2\}}            \log\frac{\exp(\mathrm{sim}(\mathbf{z}_{}^{(v)},                                          \mathbf{z}_{}^{(v^{\prime})})/\tau)}            {\sum_{k\ne(,v)}\exp(\mathrm{sim}(\mathbf{z}_{}^{(v)},                                               \mathbf{z}_{k})/\tau)},  𝐳=gϕ(fθ(𝐱̃))\mathbf{z}=g_{\phi}(f_{\theta}(\tilde{\mathbf{x}})), sim(⋅,⋅)\mathrm{sim}(\cdot,\cdot) cosine similarity τ\tau temperature. pre-training, projection head discarded backbone feature vector fθ(𝐱)f_{\theta}(\mathbf{x}) used reusable representation.

title: Pilar 4 — Contrastive Raster Embeddings as Active-Learning Covariates
text: 2025 pixels br_cerrado made centre 7×7×57\times 7\times 5 patch five normalised channels (elevation, slope, TWI, mean annual precipitation, NDVI). Reflection padding preserves original grid size. torch runtime (libtorch) available — skipping vignette.
code: library(edaphos) .torch_ok <- requireNamespace("torch", quietly = TRUE) &&              isTRUE(tryCatch(torch::torch_is_installed(),                              error = function(e) FALSE)) if (!.torch_ok) {   knitr::knit_exit(     "torch runtime (libtorch) not available — skipping vignette."   ) } data(br_cerrado, package = "edaphos")  covs_base <- c("elev", "slope", "twi", "map_mm", "ndvi") H <- length(unique(br_cerrado$y)) W <- length(unique(br_cerrado$x)) stopifnot(H * W == nrow(br_cerrado)) c(H = H, W = W) #>  H  W  #> 45 45

title: Pilar 5 — Autonomous Active Learning for Soil Mapping
text: Digital Soil Mapping (DSM) historically decoupled sampling design model fitting: pre-defined scheme collects observations model subsequently trained resulting dataset (McBratney, Mendonça Santos, Minasny 2003; Minasny McBratney 2016). static pipeline ignores fact , initial model exists, uncertainty field contains direct guidance additional information needed (Settles 2009; Brus 2019). vignette formalises Pillar 5 closed-loop Active Learning workflow shipped edaphos: algorithm iteratively () fits Quantile Regression Forest (Meinshausen 2006; Wright Ziegler 2017), (ii) computes per-candidate prediction uncertainty feature-space diversity, (iii) queries informative batch new locations (iv) refits oracle returns measured values. illustrate pipeline benchmark meuse dataset (Heuvelink Webster 2001) quantify convergence --Bag RMSE three query strategies plus logistically cost-aware variant.

title: Pilar 5 — Autonomous Active Learning for Soil Mapping
text: Let 𝒮⊂ℝ2\mathcal{S}\subset\mathbb{R}^{2} study region let 𝐱(𝐬)∈ℝp\mathbf{x}(\mathbf{s})\\mathbb{R}^{p} denote vector environmental covariates location 𝐬∈𝒮\mathbf{s}\\mathcal{S}. Assume measurable soil property y(𝐬)=f(𝐱(𝐬))+ε(𝐬)y(\mathbf{s})=f(\mathbf{x}(\mathbf{s}))+\varepsilon(\mathbf{s}), heteroscedastic error ε\varepsilon. Given labelled dataset ℒt={(𝐬i,𝐱i,yi)}=1nt\mathcal{L}_{t}=\{(\mathbf{s}_{},\mathbf{x}_{},y_{})\}_{=1}^{n_{t}} candidate pool 𝒰t⊂𝒮\ℒt\mathcal{U}_{t}\subset\mathcal{S}\setminus\mathcal{L}_{t} iteration tt, seek policy π\pi selects Bt⊂𝒰tB_{t}\subset\mathcal{U}_{t}, |Bt|=b|B_{t}|=b, maximising expected information gain finite sampling budget NN. adopt Quantile Regression Forest (QRF) (Meinshausen 2006) f̂t\hat{f}_{t}. query probability pair (τℓ,τu)∈(0,1)2(\tau_{\ell},\tau_{u})\(0,1)^{2}, QRF provides per-pixel prediction interval [q̂τℓ(𝐱),q̂τu(𝐱)]\bigl[\hat{q}_{\tau_{\ell}}(\mathbf{x}),\hat{q}_{\tau_{u}}(\mathbf{x})\bigr]; interval width ut(𝐱)=q̂τu(𝐱)−q̂τℓ(𝐱) u_{t}(\mathbf{x})\;=\;\hat{q}_{\tau_{u}}(\mathbf{x})                     \;-\;\hat{q}_{\tau_{\ell}}(\mathbf{x})  Pillar 5 uncertainty score. diversity score dt(𝐱)=min𝐱′∈ℒt∪Bt<k∥𝐳(𝐱)−𝐳(𝐱′)∥2, d_{t}(\mathbf{x})\;=\;\min_{\mathbf{x}^{\prime}\\mathcal{L}_{t}\cup B_{t}^{<k}}                       \bigl\|\mathbf{z}(\mathbf{x})-\mathbf{z}(\mathbf{x}^{\prime})\bigr\|_{2},  𝐳\mathbf{z} denotes mean-standardised covariate vector Bt<kB_{t}^{<k} candidates already greedily chosen within current batch (batch internally diverse). hybrid strategy combines convex combination 0-1 min-max-scaled scores: πhyb(𝐱;α)=αũt(𝐱)+(1−α)d̃t(𝐱),α∈[0,1]. \pi_{\text{hyb}}(\mathbf{x};\alpha)    \;=\;\alpha\,\tilde u_{t}(\mathbf{x})        +(1-\alpha)\,\tilde d_{t}(\mathbf{x}), \qquad \alpha\[0,1].  deployment autonomous samplers (drones, rovers) add logistical cost term proportional Euclidean distance base 𝐬0\mathbf{s}_{0}: πcost(𝐱;α,γ)=πhyb(𝐱;α)−γc̃(𝐱),c(𝐱)=∥𝐬(𝐱)−𝐬0∥2. \pi_{\text{cost}}(\mathbf{x};\alpha,\gamma)    \;=\;\pi_{\text{hyb}}(\mathbf{x};\alpha)        \;-\;\gamma\,\tilde c(\mathbf{x}), \qquad c(\mathbf{x})=\|\mathbf{s}(\mathbf{x})-\mathbf{s}_{0}\|_{2}. four policies dispatched strategy argument [al_query()][al_query] [al_loop()][al_loop].1

title: Pilar 5 — Autonomous Active Learning for Soil Mapping
text: meuse dataset (Heuvelink Webster 2001) holds n=155n = 155 topsoil samples Dutch floodplain; use lead concentration (mg kg−1^{-1}) target four ancillary covariates. initial labelled subset drawn conditioned Latin Hypercube Sampling (cLHS) (Minasny McBratney 2006), spreads samples across joint covariate distribution strong baseline DSM (Brus 2019).
code: library(edaphos) if (!requireNamespace("sp", quietly = TRUE)) {   knitr::knit_exit("Package `sp` not installed — skipping vignette.") } data(meuse, package = "sp")  d <- stats::na.omit(meuse[, c("x", "y", "dist", "elev", "ffreq",                               "soil", "lime", "lead")]) d$ffreq <- as.numeric(as.character(d$ffreq)) d$soil  <- as.numeric(as.character(d$soil)) d$lime  <- as.numeric(as.character(d$lime)) covs <- c("dist", "elev", "ffreq", "soil", "lime") set.seed(42) seed_idx <- al_initial_design(d, covariates = covs,                               n = 15, iter = 1000) labeled0   <- d[ seed_idx, ] candidates <- d[-seed_idx, ] c(n_labeled = nrow(labeled0), n_candidates = nrow(candidates)) #>    n_labeled n_candidates  #>           15          140

what: 3. Closed-loop fit under the hybrid policy
title: Pilar 5 — Autonomous Active Learning for Soil Mapping
code: set.seed(42) model_hybrid <- al_loop(   labeled    = labeled0,   candidates = candidates,   target     = "lead",   covariates = covs,   coords     = c("x", "y"),   budget     = 30, batch = 5,   strategy   = "hybrid", alpha = 0.7,   num.trees  = 500L, verbose = FALSE ) model_hybrid #> <edaphos_al_model> #>   target     : lead  #>   covariates : dist, elev, ffreq, soil, lime  #>   coords     : x, y  #>   n labeled  : 45  #>   iterations : 6  #>   last RMSE  : 86.96 h_hyb <- al_history(model_hybrid) h_hyb #>   iter n_labeled  rmse_oob mean_uncertainty #> 1    0        15 133.94005               NA #> 2    1        20 115.25940           490.60 #> 3    2        25 105.70449           451.92 #> 4    3        30  95.41820           382.00 #> 5    4        35  91.23926           341.60 #> 6    5        40  90.24021           260.00 #> 7    6        45  86.95717           215.00 library(ggplot2) ggplot(h_hyb, aes(n_labeled, rmse_oob)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) + geom_point(size = 2) +   labs(x = "Labelled sample size n_t",        y = expression("Out-of-Bag RMSE ("*mg~kg^{-1}*" Pb)"),        title = "Learning curve under the hybrid policy") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: Pilar 5 — Autonomous Active Learning for Soil Mapping
text: benchmark three label-dependent policies fixing cLHS seed budget (ceteris-paribus principle). Deviation curves attributable policy choice alone.  Uncertainty-sampling exploits current model maximally can cluster redundantly around single high-variance pocket; pure diversity explores broadly wastes budget regions model already handles well (Settles 2009). hybrid policy empirically dominates setting.
code: run_one <- function(strategy) {   set.seed(42)   m <- al_loop(     labeled = labeled0, candidates = candidates,     target = "lead", covariates = covs, coords = c("x", "y"),     budget = 30, batch = 5,     strategy = strategy, alpha = 0.7,     num.trees = 500L, verbose = FALSE   )   cbind(al_history(m), strategy = strategy) } h_all <- rbind(   run_one("uncertainty"),   run_one("diverse"),   run_one("hybrid") ) ggplot(h_all, aes(n_labeled, rmse_oob, colour = strategy)) +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7) + geom_point(size = 2) +   labs(x = "Labelled sample size n_t",        y = expression("OOB RMSE ("*mg~kg^{-1}*" Pb)"),        title = "Query-strategy comparison on meuse (2020 Pb topsoil)",        colour = "Strategy") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +   theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: Pilar 5 — Autonomous Active Learning for Soil Mapping
text: drone rover executes campaign, marginal cost observation uniform. "cost" strategy adds logistical penalty proportional distance base 𝐬0=(x0,y0)\mathbf{s}_{0}=(x_{0},y_{0}). decision primitive generalises -board Edge-AI pipelines oracle fielded NIR spectrometer running sampler loop closes seconds rather weeks.
code: set.seed(42) model_cost <- al_loop(   labeled = labeled0, candidates = candidates,   target = "lead", covariates = covs, coords = c("x", "y"),   budget = 30, batch = 5,   strategy = "cost",   base = c(179000, 330000),   alpha = 0.7, cost_weight = 0.4,   num.trees = 500L, verbose = FALSE ) tail(al_history(model_cost), 4) #>   iter n_labeled rmse_oob mean_uncertainty #> 4    3        30 106.8514           380.78 #> 5    4        35 120.4915           383.80 #> 6    5        40 125.0449           428.20 #> 7    6        45 119.7533           216.86

title: Pilar 5 — Autonomous Active Learning for Soil Mapping
text: hybrid strategy §3 scores every candidate alpha * uncertainty + (1 - alpha) * diversity. well-tuned heuristic, heuristic — formal objective maximising. BatchBALD (Kirsch, Amersfoort, Gal 2019) replaces heuristic information-theoretic objective: pick batch maximises mutual information labels model parameters, BatchBALD(B)=(yB;θ∣xB,𝒟).   \mathrm{BatchBALD}(B) \;=\;   \bigl(y_B ; \theta \mid x_B, \mathcal D\bigr). regression model Gaussian aleatoric noise variance σa2\sigma_a^2 epistemic posterior represented T parameter draws (QRF: T trees forest), objective reduces log-determinant: BatchBALD(B)∝12logdet(Covθ(fθ(B))+σa2I|B|).   \mathrm{BatchBALD}(B) \;\propto\;   \tfrac{1}{2}\log\det\!\bigl(     \mathrm{Cov}_\theta(f_\theta(B)) + \sigma_a^2 I_{|B|}   \bigr). log-det monotone submodular BB, greedy argmax enjoys (1−1/e)(1 - 1/e) optimality guarantee (Nemhauser, Wolsey, Fisher 1978). [al_query_batchbald()][al_query_batchbald] implementation uses incremental Cholesky / Schur updates greedy step O(m2npool)O(m^2 n_{\mathrm{pool}}) rather O(m3npool)O(m^3 n_{\mathrm{pool}}). failure mode BatchBALD specifically addresses cluster--near-duplicates pathology top-nn BALD: pool contains several candidates nearly identical covariate space, plain uncertainty-sort returns nn copies “question.” BatchBALD’s log-det penalises adding candidate whose predictive distribution overlaps strongly distribution already-selected candidate, greedy step spreads covariate space construction. Use BatchBALD () pool clustered near-duplicates, (b) QRF aleatoric noise well-estimated (default: --bag residual variance), (c) batch size moderate (n <= 50 pools ~10 000 candidates). low-budget hybrid settings also need physical-distance term, cost-aware strategy §3 remains recommended default.
code: library(edaphos) data(br_cerrado)  covs <- c("elev", "slope", "twi", "map_mm") idx  <- al_initial_design(br_cerrado, covs, n = 30L, seed = 1L) lab  <- br_cerrado[idx, ] pool <- br_cerrado[setdiff(seq_len(nrow(br_cerrado)), idx), ]  fit   <- al_fit(lab, target = "soc", covariates = covs) batch <- al_query_batchbald(fit, pool, n = 8L) head(pool[batch, covs]) #>        elev slope    twi map_mm #> 488   960.7 14.74  7.327 1717.4 #> 701  1036.3 12.94  8.071 1521.5 #> 1037 1063.5 13.74  8.888 1180.7 #> 614  1063.3  7.34  7.646 1572.5 #> 376   728.9 16.11 15.000 1402.9 #> 1886  798.1 20.00 13.578 1791.4

title: Pilar 5 — Autonomous Active Learning for Soil Mapping
text: Active Learning reframes DSM pipeline “collect, model” sequence feedback controller (Brus 2019). Three points bear emphasis: Information-theoretic coherence. Gaussian observation noise, maximising QRF interval width asymptotically equivalent maximising mutual information next observation current model — formal quantity sought Bayesian optimal design (Settles 2009). Coupling Pillar 2. model-predicted value falls outside physically admissible envelope can rejected entering greedy selection (see physics_gate argument [al_query()][al_query] Pillar 2 vignette); pre-empts pathologies uncertainty-strategies otherwise amplify. Transferability. abstraction scales benchmark datasets meuse planetary-scale products SoilGrids250m (Hengl et al. 2017); oracle implementation changes. companion vignette pilar5-soilgrids-br demonstrates identical workflow Cerrado cut-bundled edaphos. hybrid strategy §3 BatchBALD strategy §6 complementary rather substitutes: former wins logistical cost term physics gate must enter objective, latter wins priority information-theoretically optimal deduplication across clustered pool. share physics_gate interface, Pillar 2 PIML rejection rule plugs without change.

title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: High-dimensional covariate stacks — natural product modern Digital Soil Mapping pipelines (McBratney, Mendonça Santos, Minasny 2003; Wadoux, Minasny, McBratney 2020) — eventually hit classical curse dimensionality: volume covariate hypercube grows exponentially number features, number labelled soil samples remains stubbornly linear budget (Brus 2019). Pillar 6 edaphos addresses gap reformulating kernel regression problem overlap parametrised quantum states (Havlíček et al. 2019; Schuld Killoran 2019). resulting quantum kernel lives complex Hilbert space whose dimension grows 2n2^n number covariates nn, yet remains tractable simulate pure R n≤8n \le 8 subject active research program real NISQ hardware (Preskill 2018; Biamonte et al. 2017). implementation release self-contained, dependency- free pure-R state-vector simulator ZZFeatureMap circuit (Havlíček et al. 2019), quantum kernel Gram matrix evaluator, closed- form Kernel Ridge Regression (KRR) wrapper. demonstrate full pipeline binary NDVI-classification task drawn bundled br_cerrado dataset, using three covariates drive NDVI data-generating process.

what: 1. Background: the curse of dimensionality as a Hilbert-space question
title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: Classical DSM regressors (Random Forests, GAMs, Gaussian Processes) live data : features enter (possibly non-linear) kernel k(𝐱i,𝐱j)k(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) ℝn\mathbb{R}^n. RBF kernel kRBF(𝐱i,𝐱j)=exp(−γ∥𝐱i−𝐱j∥2)k_{\text{RBF}}(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) = \exp(-\gamma \lVert\mathbf{x}_i - \mathbf{x}_j\rVert^2) equivalent inner product infinite-dimensional feature space, space classically computable expressivity bounded behaviour γ\gamma. quantum feature map lifts data point 𝐱\mathbf{x} quantum state |ϕ(𝐱)⟩\lvert\phi(\mathbf{x})\rangle Hilbert space nn qubits, ℋ=ℂ2n\mathcal{H} = \mathbb{C}^{2^n}. induced quantum kernel squared overlap states: K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=|⟨ϕ(𝐱j)∣ϕ(𝐱i)⟩|2. K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \;=\;   \bigl\lvert\langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \mid \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)\rangle\bigr\rvert^{2}. carefully designed feature map, computing KK classical computer believed exponentially hard (Havlíček et al. 2019, Supplementary Information) — whole point quantum hardware evaluate overlaps space directly. small-nn problems actually arise DSM covariate stacks (typical n=4–8n = 4\text{–}8), however, classical simulation cheap, resulting kernel new scientific object study even without quantum device.

title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: Havlicek et al. feature map constructs |ϕ(𝐱)⟩\lvert\phi(\mathbf{x})\rangle parameterised unitary Uϕ(𝐱)U_\phi(\mathbf{x}) applied uniform superposition: |ϕ(𝐱)⟩=(Uϕ(𝐱)H⊗n)R|0⟩⊗n, \lvert\phi(\mathbf{x})\rangle    \;=\; \bigl(U_\phi(\mathbf{x})\,H^{\otimes n}\bigr)^{R}\,\lvert 0\rangle^{\otimes n}, R≥1R \ge 1 number encoding repetitions $$ U_\phi(\mathbf{x})   \;=\; \exp\!\Bigl(\sum_{S\subseteq[n]}\phi_S(\mathbf{x})\prod_{\S} Z_i\Bigr). $$ second-order regime non-trivial subsets singletons {}\{\} pairs {,j}\{, j\}, take ϕ{}(𝐱)=2xi,ϕ{,j}(𝐱)=2(π−xi)(π−xj). \phi_{\{\}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; 2\,x_i, \qquad \phi_{\{, j\}}(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; 2\,(\pi - x_i)(\pi - x_j). Pairwise terms implemented via standard CNOTi→jRz(ϕ{,j}(𝐱))CNOTi→j\mathrm{CNOT}_{\j}\,R_z\bigl(\phi_{\{,j\}}(\mathbf{x})\bigr)\,\mathrm{CNOT}_{\j} decomposition. exact circuit [.quantum_zz_feature_map()][quantum_feature_map] implements R/quantum_kernel.R.

title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: 8 qubits state vector length ≤256\le 256, every gate can applied O(2n)O(2^n) bit-level permutation vector — explicit 2n×2n2^n \times 2^n Kronecker product built. keeps edaphos free external quantum-simulator dependency (qsimulatR, reticulate + PennyLane) core pathway, still reproducing Havlicek Nature 2019 feature map bit--bit.
code: library(edaphos) set.seed(1)  # Sanity check: a 3-qubit state is normalised. psi <- quantum_feature_map(c(pi / 4, pi / 3, pi / 2), reps = 2L) length(psi)                 # 2^3 = 8 complex amplitudes #> [1] 8 sum(Mod(psi)^2)             # <psi|psi> = 1 #> [1] 1

what: 4. Quantum kernel on the bundled Cerrado dataset
title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: use three br_cerrado covariates drive NDVI data-generating process — slope, twi, map_mm — target binary median-split NDVI . Three qubits give Hilbert space dimension 23=82^3 = 8. features rescaled [0,π][0, \pi] [quantum_scale()][quantum_scale], de-facto range validity ZZFeatureMap. Gram matrix symmetric, K[, ] = 1 construction (self-overlap normalised state) -diagonal entries [0,1][0, 1] — numerical fingerprint valid quantum kernel.
code: data(br_cerrado, package = "edaphos")  covs <- c("slope", "twi", "map_mm")    # three drivers of NDVI in the DGP set.seed(1) idx  <- sample(nrow(br_cerrado), 200L) Xraw <- as.matrix(br_cerrado[idx, covs]) X    <- quantum_scale(Xraw)            # rescale columns -> [0, pi]  # Binary NDVI target: above / below the local median y <- sign(br_cerrado$ndvi[idx] - stats::median(br_cerrado$ndvi[idx])) y[y == 0] <- 1L table(y) #> y #>  -1   1  #> 100 100 K <- quantum_kernel(X, reps = 2L) dim(K); round(K[1:5, 1:5], 3) #> [1] 200 200 #>       [,1]  [,2]  [,3]  [,4]  [,5] #> [1,] 1.000 0.102 0.093 0.021 0.074 #> [2,] 0.102 1.000 0.088 0.042 0.102 #> [3,] 0.093 0.088 1.000 0.103 0.522 #> [4,] 0.021 0.042 0.103 1.000 0.237 #> [5,] 0.074 0.102 0.522 0.237 1.000 lambda_min <- min(eigen(K, symmetric = TRUE, only.values = TRUE)$values) stopifnot(lambda_min > -1e-8)             # positive semi-definite

what: 5. Quantum Kernel Ridge Regression on binary NDVI
title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: Gram matrix hand, Kernel Ridge Regression closed-form problem: 𝛂=(K+λI)−1𝐲. \boldsymbol{\alpha} \;=\; (K + \lambda )^{-1}\,\mathbf{y}. [quantum_krr_fit()][quantum_krr_fit] computes solve packages training covariates, dual coefficients 𝛂\boldsymbol{\alpha} kernel configuration edaphos_quantum_krr S3 object. benchmark — point scientific plumbing: every piece quantum-kernel pipeline computable hundred lines pure R, leaving mathematical physical interpretation visible every step.
code: set.seed(1) train <- sort(sample(length(y), 140L)) test  <- setdiff(seq_along(y), train)  fit <- quantum_krr_fit(   X[train, ], y[train],   reps   = 2L,   lambda = 0.1 ) fit #> <edaphos_quantum_krr> #>   n_qubits = 3   reps = 2   lambda = 0.1 #>   n_train  = 140   training RMSE = 0.6431 # Numeric scores for soft evaluation scores <- predict(fit, X[test, ], type = "numeric") # Hard labels (pm 1) via sign(scores) pred   <- predict(fit, X[test, ], type = "class") table(predicted = pred, truth = y[test]) #>          truth #> predicted -1  1 #>        -1 24 12 #>        1   5 19 mean(pred == y[test]) #> [1] 0.7166667

title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates

code: ord <- order(fit$fitted) K_sorted <- fit$K_train[ord, ord]  image(seq_len(nrow(K_sorted)), seq_len(ncol(K_sorted)),       K_sorted[, rev(seq_len(ncol(K_sorted)))],       xlab = "train sample (ordered by predicted SOC score)",       ylab = "train sample",       main = "Quantum kernel — ZZFeatureMap on br_cerrado",       col  = hcl.colors(64, "YlOrRd"),       axes = FALSE)

title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: Beyond kernel methods §1-6, Pillar 6 ships second, Qiskit-backed pipeline ground-state energy estimation soil-relevant Hamiltonians via Peruzzo et al. (2014) Variational Quantum Eigensolver (VQE) (Peruzzo et al. 2014). direct avenue “simulation materials” — predicting soil fertility first-principles energy clay-humus adsorption iron-oxide weathering complexes rather surface correlations (McClean et al. 2016a). R side exposes entire Qiskit stack four public functions, mediated reticulate:

previous_headings: 7. Variational Quantum Eigensolver for organo-mineral chemistry
title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: H2_2 molecule bond length 0.735 Å archetypal VQE benchmark. Bravyi-Kitaev-tapered Hamiltonian five Pauli terms two qubits, ground-state energy known −1.857275-1.857275 Hartree. Typical COBYLA run: VQE energy lands within 10−510^{-5} Hartree exact diagonalisation — noise-free simulator reproduces published benchmark machine precision.
code: library(edaphos)  ham_h2 <- quantum_hamiltonian_h2() ham_h2 exact_h2 <- quantum_vqe_exact(ham_h2) vqe_h2   <- quantum_vqe_fit(ham_h2, ansatz_reps = 2L,                               optimizer = "COBYLA",                               max_iter  = 200L,                               seed = 1L) vqe_h2

previous_headings: 7. Variational Quantum Eigensolver for organo-mineral chemistry
title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: quantum_hamiltonian_organo_mineral() defines deliberately small 4-qubit model nevertheless structurally similar clay-humus adsorption complex: H=−εFe(Z3+Z2)−εL(Z1+Z0)+JFeZ3Z2+JLZ1Z0+t(X3X0+X2X1), H = -\varepsilon_\mathrm{Fe}(Z_3 + Z_2)     - \varepsilon_\mathrm{L}(Z_1 + Z_0)     + J_\mathrm{Fe}\,Z_3 Z_2 + J_\mathrm{L}\,Z_1 Z_0     + t\,(X_3 X_0 + X_2 X_1), two metal-centre qubits (left pair) two ligand qubits (right pair). -site energies, -sector Z-Z exchange, cross-sector X-X hopping produce non-trivial entangled ground state written product Fe ligand wave- functions — exactly regime classical model-free regressor struggles.
code: ham_om <- quantum_hamiltonian_organo_mineral() exact_om <- quantum_vqe_exact(ham_om) vqe_om   <- quantum_vqe_fit(ham_om, ansatz_reps = 3L,                               optimizer = "COBYLA",                               max_iter  = 400L,                               seed = 1L) vqe_om library(ggplot2) df <- data.frame(iter = seq_along(vqe_om$history),                   energy = vqe_om$history) ggplot(df, aes(iter, energy)) +   geom_hline(yintercept = exact_om, linetype = 2,              colour = "firebrick") +   annotate("text", x = max(df$iter), y = exact_om,            label = sprintf("exact = %.4f", exact_om),            hjust = 1, vjust = -0.5, colour = "firebrick") +   geom_line(linewidth = 0.7, colour = "#7E1E9C") +   labs(x = "COBYLA iteration",        y = expression(paste("VQE energy estimate ", E[theta])),        title = "Pillar 6 — VQE convergence on the organo-mineral Hamiltonian") +   theme_minimal(base_size = 12)

title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: statevector simulator §7 analytic: every expectation value ⟨H⟩=⟨ψ(θ)|H|ψ(θ)⟩\langle H\rangle = \langle\psi(\theta)|H|\psi(\theta)\rangle computed contracting full 2n2^n-dimensional state vector Pauli-string Hamiltonian, COBYLA optimiser sees noise-free cost function. Real quantum hardware work like . energy evaluation reconstructed finite number projective measurements, shot noise — finite-sample variance order 1/Nshots1/\sqrt{N_\mathrm{shots}} — enters VQE loop. cost function stochastic, classical optimisers rely smooth finite-difference gradients (COBYLA, L-BFGS-B) often stall oscillate. SPSA optimiser Spall (1998) designed precisely regime: perturbs parameters simultaneously random ±1\pm 1 Rademacher vectors estimates single scalar gradient per iteration, ~parameter-count times cheaper far robust shot noise coordinate-wise differencing. v0.9.0 backend = "aer_shots" dispatch quantum_vqe_fit() runs VQE qiskit_aer.primitives.EstimatorV2 primitive, reconstructs expectation value shots Monte-Carlo samples. ansatz automatically transpiled {id,Rz,X,X,CNOT,U}\{id, R_z, \sqrt{X}, X, \mathrm{CNOT}, U\} basis gate set Aer (every IBM heavy-hex processor) speaks. 2-qubit H2_2 problem shot-based run consistently lands within milli-Hartree exact diagonalisation — two orders magnitude statevector’s residual still well inside chemical accuracy (1 kcal/mol ≈ 1.6 mHa).
code: fit_sv    <- quantum_vqe_fit(ham_h2, backend = "aer_statevector",                                optimizer = "COBYLA",                                max_iter = 200L, seed = 1L) fit_shots <- quantum_vqe_fit(ham_h2, backend = "aer_shots",                                shots    = 4096L,                                optimizer = "SPSA",                                max_iter = 80L, seed = 1L)  data.frame(   backend = c("aer_statevector", "aer_shots (4096 shots, SPSA)"),   energy  = c(fit_sv$energy,  fit_shots$energy),   gap_Ha  = c(fit_sv$gap,     fit_shots$gap) )

what: 9. IBM Quantum dispatch and hardware mitigation
title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: Running VQE real IBM Quantum processor needs three extra steps top §8. v0.9.0 automates three backend = "ibmq": Authentication backend selection. quantum_ibmq_available(), quantum_ibmq_backends() quantum_ibmq_least_busy() wrap qiskit_ibm_runtime.QiskitRuntimeService. token read IBMQ_TOKEN environment variable; -disk account configuration mutated. ISA transpilation. EfficientSU2 ansatz transpiled target backend’s coupling map native gate set using preset pass manager optimisation level 1, Pauli observable re-indexed via SparsePauliOp.apply_layout() follow routed qubit layout. Error mitigation. mitigation argument maps IBM Runtime EstimatorV2 resilience_level (Kim et al. 2023): Readout mitigation ("m3") recovers coherent-error-free expectation value inverting experimentally measured bit-flip matrix AA ⟨Ô⟩cor=−1⟨Ô⟩raw\langle \hat O\rangle_\mathrm{cor} = ^{-1}\langle \hat O\rangle_\mathrm{raw}; cheap always worth turning . ZNE amplifies noise replacing CNOT odd number folded CNOTs (effectively -op ideal gate level multiplier noise), fits resulting energies noise scale extrapolates zero — recovering coherent incoherent two-qubit-gate errors M3 . complete hybrid loop one-liner: users prefer write hybrid loop — e.g. coupling VQE result Pillar 5 Active Learning acquisition function — low-level quantum_ibmq_submit() entry point exposes single PUB submission returns expectation value, standard error IBM job id.
code: Sys.setenv(IBMQ_TOKEN = "<your-ibm-quantum-api-token>") reticulate::py_install("qiskit-ibm-runtime", pip = TRUE)  quantum_ibmq_available()              # sanity check quantum_ibmq_least_busy()             # pick the least-busy backend  fit_ibmq <- quantum_vqe_fit(   ham_h2,   backend      = "ibmq",   ibmq_backend = "ibm_brisbane",      # or NULL to let least_busy decide   shots        = 8192L,   mitigation   = "m3",   optimizer    = "SPSA",   max_iter     = 50L )

title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: 4-qubit Hamiltonian §7.2 cartoon: structural features Fe–ligand exchange problem (-site energies, -sector ZZZ Z exchange, cross-sector XXX X hopping) none chemistry. molecular geometry, atomic-orbital basis, electron-electron repulsion, Coulombic nuclear repulsion. ground-state energy just pretty number. v0.9.0, edaphos wires qiskit-nature (+ PySCF SCF driver) quantum_hamiltonian_from_pyscf() quantum_hamiltonian_organo_mineral_nature(). build genuine ab initio molecular Hamiltonian user-supplied XYZ geometry pipeline Sun et al. (2018) McClean et al. (2016b): XYZ→PySCF RHFĤAO→FreezeCoreĤact→ActiveSpace(ne,)Ĥcasci→ParityMapper(Nα,Nβ)Ĥqubit=∑kckPk. \text{XYZ} \xrightarrow{\text{PySCF RHF}} \hat H_\mathrm{AO} \xrightarrow{\text{FreezeCore}} \hat H_\mathrm{act} \xrightarrow{\text{ActiveSpace}(n_e, n_o)} \hat H_\mathrm{casci} \xrightarrow{\text{ParityMapper}(N_\alpha, N_\beta)} \hat H_\mathrm{qubit} \;=\; \sum_k c_k\,P_k. Three curated preset variants ship box. minimum viable model one piece organo-mineral chemistry matters pedology: formic acid (2e, 2o) VQE recovers tens milli-Hartree correlation energy Hartree–Fock reference — canonical signature active space adequate see static correlation frontier orbitals quantum circuit genuinely beating mean-field baseline. run custom geometry, drop preset call low-level constructor: IBM hardware SPSA + M3 + ZNE recipe §9 applies unchanged; larger active spaces (8 qubits beyond) need heavy-hex coupling map least 30 ansatz repetitions express two-body correlation structure.
code: ham_nat  <- quantum_hamiltonian_organo_mineral_nature("formic_acid") ham_nat  fit_nat  <- quantum_vqe_fit(ham_nat, ansatz_reps = 2L,                               max_iter = 200L, seed = 1L) fit_nat  # Reconstructing the total molecular energy by adding the nuclear- # repulsion, frozen-core and active-space projection shifts back. E_total <- quantum_nature_total_energy(fit_nat) E_ref   <- attr(ham_nat, "reference_energy") data.frame(   E_VQE_active_Ha = fit_nat$energy,   E_shift_Ha      = attr(ham_nat, "energy_shift"),   E_total_Ha      = E_total,   E_HF_ref_Ha     = E_ref,   correlation_mHa = 1000 * (E_total - E_ref) ) # Methane coordinated to an approximate Fe(III) hexa-aquo cluster, # 4-qubit (4e, 4o) active space, parity-mapped, STO-3G. ham_custom <- quantum_hamiltonian_from_pyscf(   atom = "Fe 0 0 0; O 2.0 0 0; H 2.7 0.7 0; H 2.7 -0.7 0; ...",   basis = "sto3g", charge = 2L, spin = 5L,   num_active_electrons = 4L, num_active_orbitals = 4L,   mapper = "parity" )

title: Pilar 6 — Quantum Kernel Methods for High-Dimensional Soil Covariates
text: pure-R simulator §1–6 scales comfortably 8 qubits (256-dim state vector; fractions second per kernel entry). Qiskit-backed VQE §7 extends ~24 qubits laptop via exact statevector simulator, ~14 qubits finite-shot Aer simulation (§8), hundreds qubits real IBM Quantum hardware v0.9.0 runtime bridge (§9), IBM-supported readout (M3) gate-folding (ZNE) mitigation. remaining frontier scientific rather infrastructural: Transition-metal active spaces. Fe(III), Mn(III/IV), Al(III) Cu(II) four cations whose coordination chemistry dominates soil organo-mineral stabilisation. Reliable VQE centres needs least (6e, 6o) active spaces correlation-consistent double-zeta basis sets (cc-pVDZ), push qubit count 12–16 circuit depth beyond non-mitigated NISQ processors can handle without significant error correction. Quantum kernels quantum_nature Hamiltonians. Reusing active-space wave function produced converged VQE feature map quantum_kernel() give Pillar 6 kernel first chemistry-aware embedding close loop §1–6 (kernel regression) §7–10 (ground-state chemistry).

title: Unified uncertainty across the six pillars
text: edaphos v1.5.0 pillar quantified uncertainty natural format: common calibration diagnostic, common plotting code, way pedometrician say “Pillar 3 map carries uncertainty Pillar 6 classifier location” uncertainty objects incommensurable. v1.6.0 introduces one class — edaphos_posterior — one calibration routine — uncertainty_calibrate() — work identically every pillar. vignette walks compact end--end example per pillar ends unified calibration table now package’s headline diagnostic.
code: library(edaphos) library(ggplot2) set.seed(20260423L)

what: 2. A single class for six posteriors
title: Unified uncertainty across the six pillars
text: Every adapter returns S3 object: autoplot method dispatches query_type:  single calibration routine uncertainty_calibrate(post, truth) returns CRPS, per-level prediction-interval coverage probability (PICP), mean prediction-interval width (MPIW), reliability data frame point RMSE — object regardless pillar produced posterior.
code: draws <- stats::rnorm(400L, mean = 1.2, sd = 0.3) post  <- edaphos_posterior(samples    = draws,                              method     = "bootstrap",                              query_type = "effect",                              units      = "g/kg per mm") post #> <edaphos_posterior> #>   method      : bootstrap #>   query_type  : effect #>   units       : g/kg per mm #>   n_samples   : 400 #>   query shape : 1 #>   mean range  : [+1.2038, +1.2038]  mean = +1.2038 #>   sd   range  : [+0.2995, +0.2995]  mean = +0.2995 autoplot(post)

what: 3.1 Pillar 1 — direct-effect posterior via block bootstrap
title: Unified uncertainty across the six pillars
text: Cluster-block bootstrap backdoor-adjusted OLS slope gives proper posterior identified direct effect.
code: skip_p1 <- !requireNamespace("dagitty", quietly = TRUE) if (!skip_p1) {   n  <- 300L   cluster <- sample(seq_len(6L), n, replace = TRUE)   x  <- stats::rnorm(n, mean = cluster * 0.5)   w  <- stats::rnorm(n)   y  <- 1.5 * x + 0.8 * w + stats::rnorm(n, sd = 0.7)   d  <- data.frame(x = x, y = y, w = w, kmeans_cluster = cluster)   dag <- dagitty::dagitty("dag { x -> y ; w -> y ; w -> x }")   post_p1 <- causal_effect_posterior(     d, dag, exposure = "x", outcome = "y",     adjustment = "w", estimator = "lm",     B = 500L, seed = 7L,     units = "y-units per x-unit"   )   # pseudo-truth for calibration: full-data point estimate   point <- as.numeric(     stats::coef(stats::lm(y ~ x + w, data = d))["x"])   calib_p1 <- uncertainty_calibrate(post_p1,                                       truth = point) }

title: Unified uncertainty across the six pillars
code: skip_p2 <- !requireNamespace("torch", quietly = TRUE) ||            !isTRUE(tryCatch(torch::torch_is_installed(),                              error = function(e) FALSE)) if (!skip_p2) {   depths <- c(5, 15, 30, 60, 100)   values <- c(25, 18, 12, 8, 6.5)   ens <- piml_neural_ode_fit_ensemble(     depths = depths, values = values,     K = 5L, hidden = c(16L, 16L), n_steps = 4L,     epochs = 200L, lr = 0.02, seed = 1L   )   post_p2 <- piml_neural_ode_posterior(     ens, newdepths = depths, units = "g/kg"   )   calib_p2 <- uncertainty_calibrate(post_p2, truth = values) }

what: 3.3 Pillar 3 — ConvLSTM ensemble forecast + Kalman posterior
title: Unified uncertainty across the six pillars
text: Uses v1.5.0 Pillar 3 bundle ships package.
code: res_path_p3 <- system.file("extdata",                              "temporal_cerrado_results.rds",                              package = "edaphos") skip_p3 <- !nzchar(res_path_p3) || !file.exists(res_path_p3) if (!skip_p3) {   R3 <- readRDS(res_path_p3)   # The bundle already carries the K=10 ConvLSTM rollout ensemble   # at the final forecast month (Dec 2023); we wrap it directly.   fc_ens_final <- R3$ensemble_forecast[,                                          R3$meta$T_future, , ]   post_p3 <- edaphos_posterior(     samples    = fc_ens_final,     method     = "ensemble",     query_type = "map",     units      = "NDVI z-units"   )   truth_p3 <- R3$truth_future[R3$meta$T_future, , ]   calib_p3 <- uncertainty_calibrate(post_p3, truth = truth_p3) }

title: Unified uncertainty across the six pillars
text: compact mock encoder keeps vignette self-contained.
code: skip_p4 <- skip_p2   # same torch dependency if (!skip_p4) {   N <- 40L; C <- 4L; P <- 8L   patches <- array(stats::rnorm(N * C * P * P), dim = c(N, C, P, P))   y_reg   <- stats::rnorm(N)   ds <- structure(     list(stack = NULL, patch_size = P, n_patches = N, n_channels = C,           means = rep(0, C), sds = rep(1, C),           valid_cells = seq_len(N),           sample = function(b) patches[sample(N, b), , , , drop = FALSE]),     class = "edaphos_tile_dataset"   )   enc <- foundation_moco_pretrain_tiles(     ds, feature_dim = 8L, proj_dim = 4L,     queue_size = 16L, batch_size = 8L, epochs = 5L,     device = "cpu", seed = 1L   )   fit4 <- foundation_finetune_ensemble(     enc, x = patches, y = y_reg, task = "regression",     K_ens = 3L, base_seed = 301L,     epochs = 10L, batch_size = 8L,     hidden = c(8L), dropout = 0.3, device = "cpu"   )   newx4 <- array(stats::rnorm(10L * C * P * P), dim = c(10L, C, P, P))   post_p4 <- as_edaphos_posterior(fit4, newx = newx4, units = "y-units")   # Pseudo-truth: ensemble-mean acts as the point estimate.   calib_p4 <- uncertainty_calibrate(post_p4, truth = post_p4$mean) }

code: skip_p5 <- !requireNamespace("ranger", quietly = TRUE) if (!skip_p5) {   n <- 160L   df <- data.frame(     x1 = stats::rnorm(n),     x2 = stats::rnorm(n),     y  = NA_real_   )   df$y <- 1.2 * df$x1 - 0.8 * df$x2 + stats::rnorm(n, sd = 0.3)   split <- sample(c(TRUE, FALSE), n, replace = TRUE, prob = c(0.75, 0.25))   fit5 <- al_fit(df[split, ], target = "y",                   covariates = c("x1", "x2"), num.trees = 300L)   post_p5 <- active_learning_posterior(fit5, df[!split, ],                                           units = "y-units")   calib_p5 <- uncertainty_calibrate(post_p5, truth = df$y[!split]) }

code: n6  <- 40L; d6 <- 3L X6  <- matrix(stats::runif(n6 * d6, 0, pi), ncol = d6) y6  <- sin(X6[, 1L]) + 0.5 * cos(X6[, 2L]) +          stats::rnorm(n6, sd = 0.15) fit6   <- quantum_krr_fit(X = X6, y = y6, reps = 1L, lambda = 0.3) # Hold out 12 test rows. Xt6  <- matrix(stats::runif(12L * d6, 0, pi), ncol = d6) yt6  <- sin(Xt6[, 1L]) + 0.5 * cos(Xt6[, 2L]) +          stats::rnorm(12L, sd = 0.15) post_p6 <- quantum_krr_posterior(fit6, newdata = Xt6,                                     n_samples = 500L,                                     units = "target-units") calib_p6 <- uncertainty_calibrate(post_p6, truth = yt6)

title: Unified uncertainty across the six pillars
text: One row per pillar, every number produced uncertainty_calibrate() routine pillar’s natural ground truth (pseudo-ground-truth true ground truth exists — typical Pillar 1 observable “true” causal effect). Unified calibration across six pillars (v1.6.0).
code: panels <- list() add_panel <- function(label, calib) {   if (is.null(calib)) return(invisible())   df <- calib$reliability_df   df$pillar <- label   panels[[length(panels) + 1L]] <<- df } if (!skip_p2) add_panel("P2 PIML",           calib_p2) if (!skip_p3) add_panel("P3 4D NDVI",        calib_p3) if (!skip_p4) add_panel("P4 foundation",     calib_p4) if (!skip_p5) add_panel("P5 AL / QRF",       calib_p5) add_panel("P6 quantum KRR",  calib_p6) if (length(panels) > 0L) {   big <- do.call(rbind, panels)   ggplot(big, aes(x = nominal, y = empirical)) +     geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0,                 linetype = "dashed", colour = "grey50") +     geom_line(colour = "#4A90E2", linewidth = 0.7) +     geom_point(colour = "#2C3E50", size = 1.6) +     facet_wrap(~ pillar, nrow = 2L) +     coord_equal(xlim = c(0, 1), ylim = c(0, 1)) +     theme_minimal(base_size = 11) +     labs(x = "nominal coverage", y = "empirical coverage",          title = "Reliability diagram per pillar") }

title: Unified uncertainty across the six pillars
text: three-line recipe now works every pillar: Two caveats flag: Pseudo-truth Pillar 1. causal effect measurable ground truth — best can plug full-data point estimate compute pseudo-PICP. number meaningful sanity check (posterior whose 95 % band misses point estimate broken) rigorous calibration assessment. Gaussian shortcuts. Pillar 6 uses analytic GP-equivalence produce posterior mean + epistemic + aleatoric SD, edaphos_posterior constructor synthesises Gaussian draws (mean, sd) available. strictly less faithful sample-based posterior true posterior skewed (Quantum-KRR rarely — kernel matrix bounded spectrum, GP posterior Gaussian).
code: post  <- as_edaphos_posterior(fit, newdata = xnew)   # or a *_posterior() helper calib <- uncertainty_calibrate(post, truth = ynew) autoplot(post); uncertainty_plot_reliability(calib)

what: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Greek ἔδαφος — “soil, ground.” edaphos research-grade R package implements frontier algorithms Digital Soil Mapping (DSM) designed extend — , regimes raw covariate stack thin, surpass — regression-tree state art [McBratney et al. 2003; Wadoux et al. 2020]. organised around six research pillars, confronting specific methodological gap contemporary literature, mathematically explicit governing object, single R function family, dedicated vignette. v1.7.0 closes circle capstone cross-pillar vignette integrates six pillars single Cerrado sampling-campaign narrative causally grounded generative DSM framework Zhang Wadoux 2026. Correlation → Causation · Black-box ML → Physics-Informed ML Static maps → 4D forecasts · Labelled-→ Foundation models Fixed sampling → Autonomous Active Learning · Classical kernels → Quantum kernels

what: Why edaphos exists: the scientific gap
title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Zhang Wadoux (2026) ask deceptively simple question: Can Digital Soil Mapping Causal? answer: “principle, yes — DSM model specifies mechanisms processes link soil-forming factors soil properties, rather relying associations .” identify three conditions must met causal inference observational data (soil surveys almost always observational): explicit causal model — DAG variables interest. Causal sufficiency — common causes exposure outcome must observed controlled. Faithfulness — independencies data must match implied causal model. distinguish two competing views causality: edaphos’s six pillars addresses one gaps concrete, reproducible computational machinery. package first R stack — taken together — operationalises generative DSM paradigm end--end, quantified uncertainty every step.

what: 🆕 v2.7.0 – v2.8.0 — Torch autograd ports + honest head-to-head WoSIS benchmark
title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Two new releases complete v2.x arc: v2.7.0 — Torch autograd backends Pilares 8, 9 10. Replaces ELM-style (hidden layers frozen, gradient head) pure-R fits full-backprop torch::optim_adam training. *_fit() gains backend = c("r", "torch") device = c("cpu", "mps", "cuda"); pure-R remains default fallback users without libtorch. proper 2-D conditional U-Net replaces MLP denoiser DDPM, GAT layer uses torch::index_add scatter softmax-per-source attention. v2.8.0 — Real head--head benchmark 1 095 WoSIS profiles. First honest cross-pillar comparison: three tie ~14.1 g/kg RMSE. P5 wins CRPS, P7 R² MPIW (-covers), P4 best-calibrated 0.89 ≈ nominal 0.90. Runner: data-raw/benchmark_wosis_p4_p5_p7.R; bundle: inst/extdata/benchmark_wosis_p4_p5_p7.rds. important P7 BHS row-alignment bug found fixed process (details NEWS.md v2.8.0).

text: naive OLS -reports NDVI’s effect SOC ~2.7× association confounded shared topographic climatic ancestors. Blocking three backdoor paths adjustmentSets() recovers identified direct effect (18.4, 95% CI [14.6, 22.3]).
code: library(edaphos) data(br_cerrado)  g   <- causal_cerrado_dag() adj <- causal_adjustment_set(g, exposure = "ndvi", outcome = "soc") adj #> [1] "map_mm" "slope"  "twi"  fit <- causal_estimate_effect(   br_cerrado, g,   exposure = "ndvi", outcome = "soc",   effect   = "direct" ) fit #> <edaphos_causal_effect> #>   ndvi -> soc #>   adjustment set : {map_mm, slope, twi} #>   direct effect  : 18.43   (95% CI: 14.61, 22.25) #>   naive effect   : 49.36   (un-adjusted, likely confounded)

previous_headings: Pillar 1 — Causal AI + LLM Knowledge Graphs
what: 🤖 From expert DAGs to LLM-driven Knowledge Graphs
title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: central scientific bottleneck causal DSM DAG construction. Hand-drawing DAG every soil study labour-intensive, subjective, rarely reproducible across researchers. Pillar 1 solves treating peer-reviewed soil literature first-class data source: Large Language Model (LLM) reads abstracts returns structured causal triples, fused expert DAG. machinery directly operationalises condition 1 Zhang & Wadoux (2026) — “explicit causal model” — scale literature instead individual expert.

previous_headings: Pillar 1 — Causal AI + LLM Knowledge Graphs > 🤖 From expert DAGs to LLM-driven Knowledge Graphs
title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: v1.5.0 default LLM backend Google DeepMind’s Gemma 4, served locally Ollama. Four properties made Gemma 4 right choice causal-KG extraction pedology: Scientific reasoning competence. Gemma 4 inherits instruction-tuned reasoning Gemma family performs well benchmarks involving multi-hop causal language (MMLU “social-sciences” & “high-school-biology” subsets). Open weights, open license. Unlike GPT Claude, Gemma 4 can audited, fine-tuned, redistributed — essential reproducible open science. Local, zero-cost inference. default gemma4:latest (~7-9 B parameters) runs laptop MPS / CPU; larger gemma4:26b runs single-GPU workstation. per-token cost means 10 k-abstract corpus extracted overnight free. Deterministic extraction format="json". Ollama’s constrained JSON output mode removes stochastic boilerplate plagues free-text LLM parsers.

previous_headings: Pillar 1 — Causal AI + LLM Knowledge Graphs > 🤖 From expert DAGs to LLM-driven Knowledge Graphs
title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: edaphos uses single tight prompt, shared across three backends exact parity: canonical-vocabulary constraint lets thousands abstracts converge clean DAG instead tangle synonyms.
code: You are a causal-inference expert annotating pedology and soil-science literature.  Your task: extract the explicit causal claims made by the passage below. Return a JSON object with a single key "claims" whose value is an array. Each array element must be an object with four fields:   - cause      : the causal variable (lower snake_case)   - effect     : the effect variable (lower snake_case)   - evidence   : a short (max 180 characters) quotation from the                  passage that supports the claim   - confidence : a number in [0, 1] reflecting how definitive the                  evidence is (0.9 = unambiguous causal phrasing,                  0.5 = suggestive, 0.2 = speculative)  Only extract claims that are EXPLICITLY SUPPORTED by the passage. Do not invent relationships. Return an empty array if none are present. Use canonical pedometric vocabulary when possible:   precipitation, mean_annual_precipitation, temperature, elevation,   slope, aspect, twi, clay, sand, silt, soc, ph, cec, bulk_density,   parent_material, land_use, vegetation, ndvi, erosion, weathering.  Output JSON object only, no prose, no markdown fences.

code: kg <- causal_kg_new()  kg <- causal_llm_ingest_abstract(   kg,   abstract = "In Cerrado Oxisols, higher mean annual precipitation               drives organic-matter accumulation; steeper slopes               enhance erosional SOC loss; long-standing native               vegetation elevates topsoil nitrogen relative to               converted pasture.",   source   = "Ferreira 2021",   backend  = "ollama",   model    = "gemma4:latest" )  causal_augment_diff(causal_cerrado_dag(),                     causal_augment_dag(causal_cerrado_dag(), kg,                                        min_confidence = 0.7)) #>                           cause              effect origin #> 1                          elev              map_mm   base #> 2                          elev               slope   base #> ... #> 14  mean_annual_precipitation                 soc     kg #> 15            steeper_slopes            soc_loss    kg #> 16         native_vegetation  topsoil_nitrogen    kg

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Three additions promote extractor demo literature-scale: Paginated corpus clients. causal_corpus_scielo() causal_corpus_openalex() page keyless public APIs, returning thousands abstracts per call; causal_corpus_deduplicate() collapses DOI + title duplicates. Resumable cached ingestion. causal_llm_ingest_corpus() takes cache_dir max_retries. Every abstract MD5-hashed extracted claims written one JSON per hash; re-runs short-circuit cache; exhausted retries recorded attr(kg, "failed"). Live AGROVOC SPARQL alignment. causal_ontology_agrovoc_align_batch() resolves KG labels FAO’s live AGROVOC SPARQL endpoint via httr2::req_perform_parallel() -disk caching — typically 5× wall-clock speedup max_active = 5, near-instant re-runs. 100-abstract production run (three OpenAlex queries, deduplicated, Gemma-4-extracted, AGROVOC-aligned) ships inst/extdata/cerrado_claims_real_corpus.jsonl reproduced end--end data-raw/run_large_corpus.R.

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: single LLM backend inherits model’s idiosyncrasies: GPT tends -generate claims, Claude -specify confidence, Gemma strictest. causal_llm_vote() runs three abstract resolves disagreements one three rules: formal resolution rule majority voting claim(c→e)∈Ccons⇔|{b∈B:(c→e)∈Cb}|≥⌈|B|/2⌉, \text{claim}\,(c \e) \C_{\text{cons}} \;\iff\;     \bigl|\{b \B \,:\, (c \e) \C_b\}\bigr| \;\ge\; \lceil |B|/2 \rceil, confidence set per-backend mean voters agreed. causal_llm_ingest_abstract_voted() runs vote adds consensus edges KG one call, tagging source paper backends agreed.
code: cons <- causal_llm_vote(   abstract = "Higher MAP drives SOC in Cerrado...",   backends = list(     list(backend = "ollama",    model = "gemma4:latest"),     list(backend = "openai",    model = "gpt-4o-mini"),     list(backend = "anthropic", model = "claude-sonnet-4-6")   ),   voting   = "majority"     # or "weighted" / "intersection" )

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Pillar 1 just -memory toy: causal_kg_save() / causal_kg_load() — serialise tidy edge list (igraph’s raw C layout), guaranteeing portability across igraph versions byte-reproducibility. causal_kg_to_turtle() — emits W3C-conformant RDF 1.1 Turtle document reified rdf:Statements, parseable rdflib, Jena, Oxigraph, Blazegraph, GraphDB Virtuoso. Pure R, zero RDF dependencies. causal_kg_rank_edges() — collapses unique (cause, effect) pairs, ranks (n_sources, mean_confidence, agrovoc_support). Answers “causal claims supported independent papers?” one call. causal_structure_learn() (v1.1.0) — bottom-DAG learning data via four bnlearn algorithms (hc, tabu, pc-stable, mmhc) whitelist / blacklist pedological priors + bootstrap edge strengths.

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: vignette("causal-discovery-trio") asks harder question: three DAG-construction strategies Pillar 1 agree? Running five methods 1 095 WoSIS profiles: major scientific finding choice DAG changes backdoor adjustment set MAP → SOC 6 covariates (LLM-augmented) 0 covariates (bnlearn mmhc) — difference order magnitude directly changes identified effect. three strategies complementary, substitutable. vignette recommends using three reporting sensitivity causal-analog observational uncertainty. 📖 Vignettes: vignette("pilar1-causal"), vignette("pilar1-causal-real"), vignette("causal-discovery-trio").

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: first quantitative validation Gemma 4 extractor powers Pillar 1. gold standard 30 Cerrado abstracts × 72 expert-annotated causal claims (inst/extdata/cerrado_gold_standard_v1.jsonl) compared extractions three backends causal_llm_extract(): Key findings. Claude best F1 (0.82) backend reaches “substantial” Cohen’s κ human annotator (0.57, Landis-Koch threshold 0.60). GPT-4o-mini fastest (2.8 s vs. 5.4 s vs. 16 s median latency per abstract). Gemma 4 free option; 10 000-abstract scale, switching Claude Gemma 4 saves ~$37.50 cost 11 F1-points. Backends disagree strongly abstracts extract (κ ≈ −0.08 0.12 pairwise), means combining via causal_llm_vote() captures complementary strengths. finding justifies three-backend architecture already shipped. benchmark runs reproducibly CI using llm_benchmark_simulate(), deterministic noise model calibrated published profiles backend. real API runs, set EDAPHOS_USE_REAL_LLM=1 provide relevant API keys. 📖 Vignette: vignette("llm-kg-benchmark").

what: v1.8.1 — Annotation tool: pre-annotation + Shiny review UI
title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: v1.8.0 benchmark runs 72-claim seed; publication-grade paper needs ~300 claims statistical power. v1.8.1 ships two-phase annotation tooling get ~3 hours: Shiny app keyboard shortcuts (n = next, = accept , 1..9 = toggle per-claim accept) trained annotator processes ~2 abstracts/minute simple cases. Every action writes disk; interrupted sessions resume exactly left . 📖 Vignette: vignette("llm-annotation-workflow").

LLM drafts one set of claims per abstract (~25 min for 100 abs.) edaphos::llm_preannotate(   corpus      = "cerrado_corpus_openalex.jsonl",   backend     = "ollama",       # or "simulator" if Ollama absent   model       = "gemma4:latest",   output_path = "draft_gold_v2.jsonl",   cache_dir   = "~/.cache/edaphos_annotation" )  # 2. Shiny reviewer — pedologist accepts / edits / rejects / adds edaphos::llm_annotation_launch(   draft_path  = "draft_gold_v2.jsonl",   output_path = "reviewed_gold_v2.jsonl",   keyboard_shortcuts = TRUE )  # 3. Export: drops rejected / untouched drafts + validates schema edaphos::llm_annotation_export(   reviewed_path = "reviewed_gold_v2.jsonl",   output_path   = "cerrado_gold_standard_v2_final.jsonl" )

text: Pillar 1 × Pillar 4 bridge. Backdoor adjustment (Pillar 1) identifies causal effects causal sufficiency — confounders observed. unobserved confounders U remain, adjusted estimator biased: β̂backdoor=βtrue+bias(U)\hat{\beta}_{\text{backdoor}} = \beta_{\text{true}} + \text{bias}(U) Instrumental variables (IVs) identify effects different set assumptions [@Wooldridge2010]: Relevance: Cov(Z,X)≠0\text{Cov}(Z, X) \neq 0 Exclusion: Z→YZ \Y XX Unconfoundedness: Z⊥UZ \perp U MoCo v2 encoder (Pillar 4), trained contrastively landscape patches without ever seeing SOC, natural IV candidate: captures landscape-scale structure orthogonal named confounders DAG. Synthetic DGP validation (known truth β = 1.5): Honest finding 1 095 real Cerrado profiles. proxy embeddings (engineered features stand-MoCo v2, still training), Stage-1 F ≈ 20 000 (instruments relevant) Sargan p < 10⁻⁹ — framework correctly flags proxy features don’t satisfy exclusion. isn’t bug; ’s framework job, demonstrates need real MoCo v2 encoder (v1.9.1) rather engineered proxies. 📖 Vignette: vignette("pilar1-pilar4-iv").
code: # 2SLS with foundation-model embeddings as instruments iv_fit <- edaphos::causal_iv_from_embeddings(   data       = cerrado_profiles,   embeddings = foundation_embeddings,    # (n x 128) MoCo v2 output   exposure   = "map",   outcome    = "soc",   covariates = c("mat","slope","elev","clay","sand","bd","trees"),   n_pcs      = 5L   # top-5 PCs as instruments ) iv_fit #> <edaphos_causal_iv> #>   map -> soc  (estimator: 2SLS, n = 1095) #>   instruments : PC_1, PC_2, PC_3, PC_4, PC_5 #>   effect      : 0.0081  (se = 0.0027, 95% CI [0.0028, 0.0134]) #>   stage-1 F   : 20014 (p < 2e-16)  partial R^2 = 0.948 #>   Sargan J    : chi2(4) = 49.4, p = 4.7e-10

Using Zenodo-published edaphos-cerrado-moco-v1 encoder 1 095 Cerrado profiles: decisive test passes. encoder pretrained contrastive self-supervision without seeing SOC produces structurally valid instruments feature engineered observed X fails exclusion. central empirical contribution v1.9.1. Honest caveat. Stage-1 F drops 7-10 (~20 000 proxies) synthetic CI-mode raster stack lacks real spatial autocorrelation SoilGrids. Running EDAPHOS_IV_REAL_STACK=1 + full 200 MB download lifts F back practically-useful range (work scoped v1.9.2).

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Sargan test validates instrument exclusion; sensitivity analysis quantifies robustness residual confounding fact. Following [@CinelliHazlett2020] compute Robustness Value (RV): minimum partial R² unobserved confounder need X Y zero estimate. Robustness table 1 095 Cerrado profiles: Scientific finding. robust causal claim Cerrado stack Clay → SOC Naive OLS (RV = 24%): plausible confounder explaining less quarter residual variance zero . MAP → SOC Backdoor OLS moderately robust (RV ≈ 8%); real-encoder IV drops ≈ 4%, synthetic-stack mode yields weak instruments (Stage-1 F ≈ 10) inflated SE. Running EDAPHOS_IV_REAL_STACK=1 full geodata download lift F reduce SE, restoring RV. package validated published Cinelli-Hazlett Theorem 4.4 example four decimal places (RV = 0.1810 expected, 0.1810 computed).
code: # Any of the following produces a RV summary s <- edaphos::causal_sensitivity_summary(effect = 0.00769,                                            se     = 0.00271,                                            df     = 1084) s$rv         # -> 0.083   (8.3% of residual variance) s$rv_alpha   # -> 0.026   (threshold for significance loss)  # Or wrap an `lm` / `edaphos_causal_iv`: edaphos::causal_sensitivity_from_lm(fit, exposure = "map") edaphos::causal_sensitivity_from_iv(iv_fit)

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Classical depth-harmonisation uses equal-area splines [Bishop et al. 1999] — purely mathematical object disconnected pedogenesis. Pillar 2 replaces spline physics-informed kinetic clay illuviation / organic-matter decay, parameterised either analytically neural network integrated end--end. Alignment Zhang & Wadoux (2026). pedogenetic ODE purest form generative causality: soil property arises process explicitly specified modeller. simultaneously satisfies conditions 1 (explicit causal model) 3 (faithfulness), gives pedologist mechanistic handle variable-importance metrics never deliver.

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Depth-dependent translocation toward parent-material asymptote, dydz=−λ0e−μz(y(z)−y∞)(parametric)ordydz=fθ(z,y,𝐱)(Neural ODE). \frac{dy}{dz} \;=\; -\,\lambda_0\,e^{-\mu z}\,\bigl(y(z) - y_\infty\bigr) \qquad\text{(parametric)} \qquad\text{}\qquad \frac{dy}{dz} \;=\; f_\theta\bigl(z, y, \mathbf{x}\bigr) \qquad\text{(Neural ODE).} parametric form integrated deSolve::lsoda; Neural ODE form fixed-step Runge-Kutta integrator torch training back-propagates whole trajectory [Chen et al. 2018].

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: fitted parameters pedological interpretation: y0 ≈ 23 % clay surface horizon, y_inf ≈ 62 % argillic-horizon asymptote, mu tells us whether translocation accelerates slows depth. Neural ODE fits four horizons tighter RMSE default non-monotonic profiles (E--, buried paleosols).
code: data(sp4, package = "aqp") sp4$depth <- (sp4$top + sp4$bottom) / 2 colusa <- subset(sp4, id == "colusa")  # Point estimate param  <- piml_profile_fit(colusa$depth, colusa$clay)  # Bayesian posterior (Laplace by default; adaptive RWM available) fit    <- piml_profile_fit_bayesian(colusa$depth, colusa$clay,                                      method = "laplace", seed = 1L)  # Neural ODE neural <- piml_neural_ode_fit(   colusa$depth, colusa$clay,   hidden = c(16L, 16L), epochs = 500L, seed = 1L )  # Deep ensemble (K = 5) — canonical uncertainty for the Neural ODE ens <- piml_neural_ode_fit_ensemble(colusa$depth, colusa$clay, K = 5L)  predict(fit, newdepths = c(10, 20, 40, 80),         interval = 0.95, include_obs_noise = TRUE, seed = 1L)

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: al_physics_gate_piml() takes PIML fit turns rejection gate Pillar 5 Active Learning loop uses discard candidates whose predicted profile violates monotonicity, non-negativity, plausible-range constraints. concrete implementation “process-informed filter” Zhang & Wadoux (2026) advocate: model candidates aren’t just ranked statistical uncertainty also physical plausibility. 📖 Vignette: vignette("pilar2-piml-profile").

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: digital soil maps report time-invariant field, ecologically false: topsoil SOC responds measurably climate forcing monthly--annual scales [Lehmann Kleber 2015; Minasny et al. 2017]. Zhang Wadoux (2026) explicitly flag weakness successionist DSM: “soil measurements observations capture snapshot emerging complex, dynamic system, without clear temporal sequencing.” Pillar 3 adds time dimension stacked Convolutional LSTM [Shi et al. 2015] stochastic Ensemble Kalman Filter [Evensen 1994] assimilating new -situ observations.

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: multi-layer ConvLSTM propagates spatial hidden state 𝐇t\mathbf{H}_t cell state 𝐂t\mathbf{C}_t alongside time: 𝐢t=σ(Wxi*𝐗t+Whi*𝐇t−1),𝐟t=σ(Wxf*𝐗t+Whf*𝐇t−1),𝐠t=tanh(Wxg*𝐗t+Whg*𝐇t−1),𝐨t=σ(Wxo*𝐗t+*𝐇t−1),𝐂t=𝐟t⊙𝐂t−1+𝐢t⊙𝐠t,𝐇t=𝐨t⊙tanh(𝐂t), \begin{aligned} \mathbf{}_t &= \sigma\bigl(W_{xi} * \mathbf{X}_t + W_{hi} * \mathbf{H}_{t-1}\bigr), \\ \mathbf{f}_t &= \sigma\bigl(W_{xf} * \mathbf{X}_t + W_{hf} * \mathbf{H}_{t-1}\bigr), \\ \mathbf{g}_t &= \tanh\bigl(W_{xg} * \mathbf{X}_t + W_{hg} * \mathbf{H}_{t-1}\bigr), \\ \mathbf{o}_t &= \sigma\bigl(W_{xo} * \mathbf{X}_t + W_{ho} * \mathbf{H}_{t-1}\bigr), \\ \mathbf{C}_t &= \mathbf{f}_t \odot \mathbf{C}_{t-1} + \mathbf{}_t \odot \mathbf{g}_t, \\ \mathbf{H}_t &= \mathbf{o}_t \odot \tanh(\mathbf{C}_t), \end{aligned} * 2-D convolution ⊙ Hadamard product.

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
code: cube   <- temporal_synth_soc_cube(H = 12L, W = 12L, T_total = 18L, seed = 7L) past   <- temporal_cube_to_tensor(cube, t_slice = 1:12) future <- temporal_cube_to_tensor(cube, t_slice = 13:18)  fit <- temporal_convlstm_fit(   past$sequence, past$target,   hidden_dims     = c(12L, 6L),        # stacked ConvLSTM   kernel_size     = 3L,   return_sequence = TRUE,   epochs          = 120L, lr = 0.02,   seed            = 1L ) forecast <- temporal_convlstm_rollout(   fit,   past_sequence  = past$sequence,   future_drivers = future$sequence )

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: realistic DSM pipeline, new -situ observations keep arriving ConvLSTM trained. temporal_kalman_update() nudges trained forecast toward new observations stochastic Ensemble Kalman Filter [Evensen 1994; Burgers, van Leeuwen Evensen 1998]: gain matrix estimated directly ensemble sample covariance applied per-member, posterior ensemble reflects new evidence without retraining. default localization Gaspari-Cohn — compactly supported 5th-order polynomial tapers gain smoothly zero twice specified radius, preventing spurious long-range correlations typical small ensembles.
code: fc_ens <- array(..., dim = c(K = 10, H = 10, W = 10))  assim <- temporal_kalman_update(   forecast_ensemble   = fc_ens,   obs_value           = c(0.62, 0.58, 0.51),   obs_row             = c(5L, 10L, 15L),   obs_col             = c(5L, 10L, 15L),   obs_sd              = 0.02,   localization_radius = 2                    # Gaspari-Cohn taper )

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Labelled soil samples expensive; covariate rasters abundant. Self-Supervised Learning (SSL) [Chen et al. 2020; Jean et al. 2019] pre-trains encoder unlabelled raster patches reuses resulting representation learned feature map — premise future “SoilGPT”.

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: batch BB raster patches, SimCLR draws two stochastic augmentations per patch, encodes shared backbone CNN projection head, minimises normalised temperature-scaled cross-entropy loss [Chen et al. 2020]: ℒNT-Xent=−12B∑=1B∑v∈{1,2}logexp(sim(𝐳i(v),𝐳i(v′))/τ)∑k≠(,v)exp(sim(𝐳i(v),𝐳k)/τ). \mathcal{L}_{\text{NT-Xent}} \;=\; -\frac{1}{2B} \sum_{=1}^{B}\sum_{v\\{1,2\}} \log \frac{\exp\!\bigl(\mathrm{sim}(\mathbf{z}^{(v)}_{},\,\mathbf{z}^{(v')}_{}) / \tau\bigr)}      {\displaystyle\sum_{k \neq (,v)} \exp\!\bigl(\mathrm{sim}(\mathbf{z}^{(v)}_{},\,\mathbf{z}_{k})/\tau\bigr)}. pre-training projection head discarded backbone output fθ(𝐱)f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) reused downstream feature vector. MoCo v2 [et al. 2020; Chen et al. 2020b] replaces two-view symmetric loss queue-based momentum encoder, scaling larger batches.

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: first published encoder — edaphos-cerrado-moco-v1 — pre-trained Apple M1 Max (MPS) 50 000 16 × 16 tiles core Cerrado AoI (longitude −53 −43, latitude −23 −10), stacking SoilGrids 250 m, WorldClim 2.1 SRTM 30-arc-second. Zenodo DOI 10.5281/zenodo.19701276. encoder v2 (200 k steps, 10× v1 budget) currently training; v1.3.2 re-benchmark new weights.
code: moco <- foundation_weights_load("edaphos-cerrado-moco-v1", verbose = TRUE) fit  <- foundation_fit_classifier(   moco, labelled_patches, soil_order,   freeze_backbone = TRUE, head = "linear",   epochs = 40L, device = "mps", seed = 1L ) predict(fit, new_patches, type = "prob")

Pillar 5 closes loop: fit → query → measure → refit.

text: hybrid batch-mode acquisition policy, πhyb(𝐱;α)=αũt(𝐱)+(1−α)d̃t(𝐱),α∈[0,1], \pi_{\text{hyb}}(\mathbf{x};\alpha)  \;=\;\alpha\,\tilde{u}_t(\mathbf{x}) \;+\; (1-\alpha)\,\tilde{d}_t(\mathbf{x}), \qquad \alpha\[0,1], ũt\tilde{u}_t Quantile Regression Forest [Meinshausen 2006] prediction-interval width d̃t\tilde{d}_t min-distance feature-space diversity already-labelled set. "cost" variant adds logistical-distance penalty autonomous sampler deployment; physics_gate keyword rejects physically implausible candidates via PIML fit (Pillar 2 × 5 bridge).

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: OOB RMSE falls 5.37 4.94 g/kg nine batches 5 samples. Queried points concentrate transition zones covariate regimes — exactly expert pedologists historically placed transects.
code: covs <- c("elev", "slope", "twi", "map_mm", "ndvi")  set.seed(42) seed_idx <- al_initial_design(br_cerrado, covariates = covs, n = 25L, iter = 1500L)  set.seed(42) model <- al_loop(   labeled    = br_cerrado[ seed_idx, ],   candidates = br_cerrado[-seed_idx, ],   target     = "soc", covariates = covs,   coords     = c("x", "y"),   budget     = 45L, batch = 5L,   strategy   = "hybrid", alpha = 0.7,   num.trees  = 500L, verbose = FALSE ) tail(al_history(model), 5) #>    iter n_labeled rmse_oob mean_uncertainty #> 6     5        50 5.387328          20.1760 #> 7     6        55 5.376662          19.1320 #> 8     7        60 5.294309          19.1900 #> 9     8        65 4.913287          18.3640 #> 10    9        70 4.942248          16.9916

QRF (al_fit() backbone) trees parameter samples, joint covariance epistemic posterior per-tree empirical covariance across candidates. Gaussian aleatoric noise σa2\sigma_a^2 objective reduces log-determinant (1−1/e)(1 - 1/e) optimality guarantee [Nemhauser et al. 1978]. Incremental Cholesky / Schur-complement updates make every greedy step O(m2⋅npool)O(m^2 \cdot n_{\text{pool}}). 📖 Vignettes: vignette("pilar5-active-learning"), vignette("pilar5-soilgrids-br").

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: High-dimensional covariate stacks eventually hit classical curse dimensionality. Pillar 6 reformulates kernel regression problem overlap parametrised quantum states [Havlicek et al. 2019; Schuld Killoran 2019], living Hilbert space dimension 2n2^n number qubits yet classically simulable pure R n≤8n \le 8.

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: ZZFeatureMap quantum state |ϕ(𝐱)⟩=(Uϕ(𝐱)H⊗n)R|0⟩⊗n \lvert\phi(\mathbf{x})\rangle \;=\;   \bigl(U_\phi(\mathbf{x})\,H^{\otimes n}\bigr)^{R}\,\lvert 0\rangle^{\otimes n} induces kernel K(𝐱i,𝐱j)=|⟨ϕ(𝐱j)∣ϕ(𝐱i)⟩|2, K(\mathbf{x}_i, \mathbf{x}_j) \;=\;   \bigl\lvert \langle \phi(\mathbf{x}_j) \mid \phi(\mathbf{x}_i)\rangle \bigr\rvert^{2}, $$ U_\phi(\mathbf{x}) \;=\; \exp\!\Bigl(\!\sum_{S\subseteq[n]} \phi_S(\mathbf{x})\!\prod_{\S}\! Z_i\Bigr), \quad \phi_{\{\}}(\mathbf{x}) = 2\,x_i, \quad \phi_{\{,j\}}(\mathbf{x}) = 2\,(\pi - x_i)(\pi - x_j). $$

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Using well-known equivalence Kernel Ridge Regression Gaussian-process regression [Rasmussen & Williams 2006 §2.3], Q-KRR’s predictive variance analytic: σϵ2(𝐱⋆)=k(𝐱⋆,𝐱⋆)⏟aleatoric−k(𝐱⋆,X)(K+λI)−1k(X,𝐱⋆)⏟epistemic+σn2 \sigma_\epsilon^2(\mathbf{x}_\star) = \underbrace{k(\mathbf{x}_\star, \mathbf{x}_\star)}_{\text{aleatoric}} - \underbrace{k(\mathbf{x}_\star, X)\,(K + \lambda )^{-1}\,k(X, \mathbf{x}_\star)}_{\text{epistemic}} + \sigma_n^2 σn2\sigma_n^2 leave-one-residual variance. quantum_krr_posterior() wraps edaphos_posterior epistemic / aleatoric decomposition carried object.

title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Three interchangeable execution backends variational quantum eigensolvers: Three organo-mineral Hamiltonians come pre-built via qiskit-nature + PySCF:  📖 Vignette: vignette("pilar6-quantum").
code: ham <- quantum_hamiltonian_h2()  # 1) Exact noiseless statevector quantum_vqe_fit(ham, backend = "aer_statevector", seed = 1L)  # 2) Shot-based Aer simulation with SPSA quantum_vqe_fit(ham, backend = "aer_shots",                 shots = 4096L, optimizer = "SPSA",                 max_iter = 80L, seed = 1L)  # 3) Real IBM Quantum hardware with M3 readout mitigation Sys.setenv(IBMQ_TOKEN = "<your-ibm-quantum-api-token>") quantum_vqe_fit(ham, backend = "ibmq",                 ibmq_backend = "ibm_brisbane",                 shots = 8192L, mitigation = "m3",                 optimizer = "SPSA", max_iter = 50L)

what: v2.0.0 — Quantum kernel over foundation-model embeddings
title: edaphos — Frontier algorithms for Digital Soil Mapping
text: Pilar 4 × Pilar 6 fusion. Foundation embeddings (64-dim MoCo v2 outputs) compressed n_pcs principal components rescaled [−π,π][-\pi, \pi], lifted 2n2^n-dimensional Hilbert space ZZFeatureMap. Four new exported functions: 5-fold spatial CV 1 095 Cerrado profiles (synthetic-stack mode): Honest finding. synthetic embeddings (random Gaussian rasters), quantum lift foundation PCs matches surpass ranger. four regressors statistically indistinguishable benchmark. Three testable explanations v2.0.x: Synthetic stack carries real spatial signal → encoder sees noise → embeddings uninformative. Test: EDAPHOS_IV_REAL_STACK=1. Encoder v1 undertrained (20k InfoNCE). Test: v2 (200k steps, currently training). 6 qubits . Test: scale 8–10 qubits. contribution infrastructure. qf_* functions formalise Pilar 4 × Pilar 6 fusion ship reproducible benchmark decides question empirically richer inputs become available. 📖 Vignette: vignette("pilar4-pilar6-quantum").
code: # 1. Reduce foundation embeddings to top-n PCs in quantum range red <- edaphos::qf_embed_reduce(embeddings, n_pcs = 6L)  # 2. Compare quantum, RBF, and linear Gram matrices cmp <- edaphos::qf_kernel_compare(red$X_q, reps = 2L) cmp$diagnostics   # Frobenius distance + effective rank  # 3. Fit Quantum-KRR directly on foundation embeddings fit <- edaphos::qf_krr_fit(embeddings, y,                             n_pcs = 6L, reps = 2L, lambda = 0.5)  # 4. 4-way head-to-head benchmark bm <- edaphos::qf_krr_benchmark(embeddings, covariates, y,                                   n_pcs = 6L, reps = 2L, lambda = 0.5)

Required "cost" query strategy. num.trees Integer, number trees (default 500). ... Additional arguments forwarded ranger::ranger().
